tradition_baseline: Plymouth Brethren-adjacent — classical Reformed Protestant orthodoxy + Brethren distinctives
generated_by: Five parallel Opus subagents, one per category slot of the brethren-doctrine personal-beliefs taxonomy.
discernment_principle: Doctrinal precision and visible fruit are independent axes. tier=essential errors break fellowship absolutely. tier=convictional errors break membership but allow visit/participate. tier=preference differences MUST NOT outweigh demonstrable Christian fruit (love, joy, welcome) when discerning a local church. The point of this file is calibrated discernment, not scoring churches.
usage: Each entry classifies one doctrine or practice. KPIs answer distinct questions: tier (overall weight), martyrdom_value (would-die-for scale), cult_marker (cult-grade diagnostic), evangelism_target (gospel-status diagnostic), and an escalating relationship ladder: visit < participate < serve_in_ministry < member, plus marry, teach_to_children, correct, break_fellowship. Use this file to triage a church statement of faith or sermon claim against personal baseline.

field_definitions.id: Stable kebab-case slug. Prefix doc- for doctrine, prc- for practice, cult- for cult marker, het- for heterodoxy marker.
field_definitions.subcategory: Optional finer grouping; null if not applicable.
field_definitions.statement: One-sentence affirmation of the position (phrased as the orthodox/affirming claim so denial is clear).
field_definitions.personal_position: affirm | deny | open | uncertain — owner stance on the statement.
field_definitions.position_rationale: One to two sentences, scripturally and traditionally grounded.
field_definitions.scripture_anchors: Primary biblical passages supporting the position.
field_definitions.confessional_anchors: Creeds/confessions affirming this (Nicene, Chalcedon, Westminster, 1689 Baptist, etc.).
field_definitions.historical_consensus: orthodox = Nicene/Chalcedonian + Magisterial Reformation consensus | divided = legitimate Protestant disagreement | minority = real but smaller stream | heterodox = outside historic consensus, not formally cult | heretical = formally condemned in early creeds.
field_definitions.brethren_distinctive: true if this position is distinctive to the Brethren tradition (vs. broadly held by orthodox Christianity).
field_definitions.tier: essential = core gospel/Trinitarian boundary | convictional = clear scripture, would not compromise, church-membership level | important = matters but will not break fellowship | preference = methodological/practical | adiaphora = indifferent, freedom of conscience.
field_definitions.cult_marker_if_denied: Denial flags the holder/group as cult-grade. Reserved for denial of Trinity, full deity/humanity of Christ, bodily resurrection, justification by faith alone, addition of Scripture-level revelation, salvation by works alone.
field_definitions.evangelism_target_if_denied: Denial implies the person is likely outside saving faith and needs the gospel.
field_definitions.break_fellowship_if_denied: Refuse ongoing close fellowship over this.
field_definitions.visit_if_taught_otherwise: Okay to visit a church teaching the opposite position.
field_definitions.participate_if_taught_otherwise: Okay to take part in worship/communion at such a church.
field_definitions.serve_in_ministry_if_held_otherwise: Would volunteer/teach/lead under leadership holding the opposite position.
field_definitions.member_if_taught_otherwise: Could become a formal member at such a church.
field_definitions.marry_if_held_otherwise: Could marry someone holding the opposite position.
field_definitions.teach_to_children: Would actively teach this position to my children.
field_definitions.correct_if_encountered: Must attempt correction when encountered (vs. let it pass).
field_definitions.notes: Free-form context, fruit-vs-precision nuance, named cults/movements where applicable.

schema.relationship_ladder: visit (least committed) < participate < serve_in_ministry < member (most committed). Each level is an independent boolean — true at one level does not imply true at the next, and vice versa.

tier_definitions.essential: Core gospel and Trinitarian boundaries. Denial = false gospel or cult-grade error. Always teach to children, always correct, never become member where denied.
tier_definitions.convictional: Clear scriptural conviction. Membership-level threshold. Would not compromise on doctrine, but visit/participate is usually fine where denied.
tier_definitions.important: Matters significantly but does not break fellowship. Reservations about membership, but not absolute. Marry across is usually fine.
tier_definitions.preference: Methodological or practical. Membership across is fine. Worship style, communion frequency, calendar customs.
tier_definitions.adiaphora: Truly indifferent. Freedom of conscience. All thresholds permissive.

entries.statement: No revelation given after the apostolic age binds the conscience or stands on par with Scripture.
entries.position_rationale: The canon is closed; Hebrews 1:1-2 marks Christ as God's final word and the apostolic foundation is laid once (Eph 2:20). Brethren tradition holds this firmly.
entries.notes: This targets LDS (Book of Mormon), Watchtower, and groups elevating prophets' words to canon-equivalent. Charismatic 'words of knowledge' that don't claim canon-status are addressed in slot 5.

entries.statement: Scripture is the final and supreme authority for the Church, above all tradition, councils, and personal experience.
entries.position_rationale: Sola Scriptura is the formal principle of the Reformation and a defining Brethren commitment; tradition and experience are weighed by Scripture, never above it.
entries.notes: Adding revelation that binds conscience equally with Scripture (Catholic Magisterium, LDS, Watchtower) crosses into cult-marker territory under doc-ongoing-revelation-binding.

entries.statement: Scripture in the original autographs is without error in all that it affirms, including history, science, and theology.
entries.position_rationale: If God cannot lie (Titus 1:2) and Scripture is His word, errors are categorically excluded; this is the Brethren-tradition baseline and the historic Protestant position.
entries.notes: Denial places one in liberal Protestantism; not cult-grade but undermines all downstream doctrine.

entries.statement: Scripture cannot fail to accomplish what God intends and is incapable of misleading in matters of faith and practice.
entries.position_rationale: Isaiah 55:11 and Matthew 5:18 anchor Scripture's unfailing efficacy; this is closely tied to inerrancy in Brethren teaching.

entries.statement: All Scripture is verbally and plenarily inspired by God, such that the very words of the original autographs are God-breathed.
entries.position_rationale: 2 Timothy 3:16 grounds inspiration in the text itself, not merely the authors or ideas; the Brethren tradition holds verbal-plenary inspiration as foundational to all other doctrine.
entries.notes: Adding revelation on par with Scripture (Mormonism, Islam) is cult-grade; denial within professing Christianity is heterodox liberalism.

entries.statement: Scripture is sufficient for faith, doctrine, and practice — no further revelation is needed for salvation or godliness.
entries.position_rationale: 2 Timothy 3:16-17 explicitly equips the believer for every good work; sola Scriptura is foundational to Protestant and Brethren identity.
entries.notes: Key contrast with Roman Catholic Tradition+Magisterium and with charismatic ongoing-revelation claims that bind conscience.

entries.statement: The biblical canon comprises the 39 Old Testament and 27 New Testament books recognized by the Protestant tradition.
entries.position_rationale: The Hebrew canon Jesus referenced (Luke 24:44) and the apostolic-era reception of the 27 NT books form the basis; Brethren follow the Protestant 66-book canon.
entries.notes: Catholic and Orthodox include Apocrypha/Deuterocanon — divided across Christianity but settled within Protestantism.

entries.statement: Errantism — the view that Scripture contains real errors in matters of history, science, or doctrine — is to be rejected.
entries.position_rationale: Errantism functionally subordinates Scripture to human judgment about which parts are reliable; Brethren tradition rejects this.
entries.notes: Diagnostic entry — names the deviation. Many mainline Protestant churches teach this.

entries.statement: Scripture is sufficiently clear in matters necessary for salvation that any literate believer with the Spirit's help can grasp them.
entries.position_rationale: Deuteronomy 6 and Psalm 119:130 assume ordinary believers can read and obey God's word; Brethren tradition strongly emphasizes lay reading.
entries.notes: Denial supports clericalism and gnostic readings; Brethren reject any 'priestly class' as sole interpreters.

entries.statement: The Apocryphal/Deuterocanonical books are not Scripture and are not authoritative for doctrine, though they may have historical value.
entries.position_rationale: Never cited as Scripture by Christ or the apostles, never in the Hebrew canon; Brethren follow the Reformed Protestant rejection.
entries.notes: Reading the Apocrypha as historical/edifying literature is acceptable; treating it as inspired Scripture is the divide.

entries.statement: God is utterly holy — set apart in moral purity and majestic transcendence.
entries.position_rationale: Isaiah 6 and Revelation 4:8 frame holiness as God's central self-revelation; ground of all biblical ethics and worship.
entries.notes: Churches that minimize God's holiness in favor of love-only sentimentality fail this attribute test.

entries.statement: God is love in His very essence, eternally loving within the Trinity and toward His creatures.
entries.position_rationale: 1 John 4:8,16 grounds love as constitutive of God's being; this love is never opposed to His holiness or justice but unified in the simple divine essence.
entries.notes: Doctrinally precise churches that lack visible love fail to embody this attribute even while affirming it.

entries.statement: God is all-powerful, able to do all that He wills, limited only by His own nature.
entries.position_rationale: Genesis 17:1, Job 42:2, and Luke 1:37 affirm omnipotence; God cannot do the logically impossible or contradict His character (e.g., lie, deny Himself).

entries.statement: God knows all things, actual and possible, past, present, and future, exhaustively and eternally.
entries.position_rationale: Psalm 139, Isaiah 46:9-10, and 1 John 3:20 affirm exhaustive divine knowledge including future free acts; Brethren and historic Protestantism affirm this.
entries.notes: Open theism denies exhaustive foreknowledge — see separate entry.

entries.statement: God sovereignly rules over all things, working all events according to the counsel of His will.
entries.position_rationale: Ephesians 1:11 and Daniel 4:35 ground exhaustive sovereignty; Brethren typically affirm sovereignty without taking strict-Calvinist exhaustive-determinism positions.
entries.notes: The Calvinist-Arminian debate over the mode of sovereignty belongs in slot 3 (soteriology), not here.

entries.statement: The one true and living God exists eternally as the self-existent creator and sustainer of all things.
entries.position_rationale: Genesis 1:1 and Romans 1:19-20 ground both special and general revelation of God's existence; the foundational confession of all monotheism.
entries.notes: Atheism is the denial; without this nothing else stands.

entries.statement: Modalism — the view that Father, Son, and Spirit are merely modes or roles of one person — is to be rejected as heretical.
entries.position_rationale: The simultaneous distinction of persons at Christ's baptism (Matt 3:16-17) and the Son praying to the Father refute modalism; condemned formally in the early church.
entries.notes: Oneness Pentecostalism (UPCI, T.D. Jakes historically) is the modern modalist movement — diagnostic for cult-adjacent classification.

entries.statement: God exists eternally as one being in three co-equal, co-eternal, distinct persons: Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.
entries.position_rationale: Matthew 28:19, 2 Corinthians 13:14, and the Johannine prologue ground the triune confession; the Nicene-Chalcedonian definition is the orthodox boundary.
entries.notes: Trinity-denial is the universal cult marker — Watchtower, LDS, Oneness Pentecostalism, Unitarianism.

entries.statement: Tritheism — the view that the three persons are three separate gods — is to be rejected as heretical.
entries.position_rationale: The Shema (Deut 6:4) and the consistent monotheism of Scripture forbid three-gods readings; orthodox Trinity is one being, three persons.
entries.notes: LDS theology (separate beings, exalted men become gods) is functionally tritheist/polytheist — clear cult marker.

entries.statement: God is self-existent, deriving His being from nothing outside Himself.
entries.position_rationale: Exodus 3:14 ('I AM') and John 5:26 ground God's life-in-Himself; aseity distinguishes the Creator from all creatures.
entries.notes: Process theology denies aseity; LDS doctrine of God-as-exalted-man also denies it.

entries.statement: God is unchanging in His being, perfections, purposes, and promises.
entries.position_rationale: Malachi 3:6, James 1:17, and Hebrews 13:8 ground immutability; anthropomorphic 'repentance' texts describe relational change toward changed creatures, not God's essence.
entries.notes: Process theology and open theism deny strong immutability.

entries.statement: God is fully present everywhere, not bounded by space, while remaining distinct from creation.
entries.position_rationale: Psalm 139:7-10 and Jeremiah 23:24 ground omnipresence; this rules out both deism (absent God) and pantheism (God = creation).

entries.statement: Open Theism — the view that God does not exhaustively know future free human actions — is to be rejected.
entries.position_rationale: Isaiah 46:9-10 grounds God's declaring 'the end from the beginning' as His distinctive deity; specific predictive prophecies (e.g., Cyrus by name, Peter's denial) require exhaustive foreknowledge.
entries.notes: Diagnostic entry for open theism (Pinnock, Boyd, Sanders). Heterodox, not formally heretical, but redefines God.

entries.statement: God eternally decrees whatsoever comes to pass, in a way consistent with creaturely responsibility.
entries.position_rationale: Acts 2:23, Acts 4:27-28, and Ephesians 1:11 hold divine decree and human responsibility together; Brethren tradition affirms decrees while resisting hyper-determinism.
entries.notes: The exact mode of decree (compatibilist vs. simple-foreknowledge) is divided across orthodox traditions.

entries.statement: God is not composed of parts; His attributes are identical with His essence.
entries.position_rationale: Classical theism's confession; God's love, holiness, and being are not separable parts but the one simple divine essence considered from different angles.
entries.notes: Some modern evangelicals reject classical simplicity; this remains a serious but not church-dividing matter for me.

entries.statement: Christ ascended bodily into heaven forty days after His resurrection.
entries.position_rationale: Acts 1:9-11 and Luke 24:50-51 record the bodily ascension; required for the heavenly session and the promise of bodily return.

entries.statement: Jesus Christ rose bodily from the dead on the third day.
entries.position_rationale: 1 Corinthians 15:3-8, Luke 24:39, and the empty-tomb accounts ground bodily resurrection; without it our faith is futile (1 Cor 15:17).
entries.notes: Spiritual-only resurrection (Watchtower, Bultmann) is heretical/cult-grade. Liberal denial is heterodox.

entries.statement: Jesus Christ is fully and truly God, of one substance (homoousios) with the Father.
entries.position_rationale: John 1:1, John 20:28, Colossians 2:9, and Titus 2:13 affirm Christ's full deity; the Nicene boundary against Arianism is non-negotiable.
entries.notes: Watchtower (Christ as Michael/created), LDS (Christ as elder brother of Lucifer), Arianism — all cult-grade.

entries.statement: Jesus Christ is fully and truly human, taking on a complete human nature including body and rational soul.
entries.position_rationale: John 1:14, Hebrews 2:14-17, and 1 John 4:2-3 ground full humanity; denial is anti-Christ per John's letter and undermines the atonement.
entries.notes: Docetism, Apollinarianism (no human soul), and modern docetic Christologies are excluded.

entries.statement: Jesus Christ was without sin in His thoughts, words, and deeds throughout His earthly life.
entries.position_rationale: Hebrews 4:15, 2 Corinthians 5:21, and 1 Peter 2:22 affirm Christ's sinlessness; required for substitutionary atonement.
entries.notes: Denial collapses the gospel — a sinful Christ cannot save.

entries.statement: Christ is the sole mediator between God and humanity; no saint, angel, or Mary mediates access to God.
entries.position_rationale: 1 Timothy 2:5 explicitly names one mediator; this is a hallmark Brethren and Reformation conviction against Marian and saintly intercession as mediation.
entries.notes: Roman Catholic and Eastern Orthodox practice of saintly/Marian intercession violates this; visiting is fine, membership/serving is not.

entries.statement: The Son is eternally the Son — co-eternal and co-equal with the Father — not adopted or begun in time.
entries.position_rationale: John 1:1-2, John 17:5, and Hebrews 1:8 affirm the Son's pre-existence as Son; Nicene 'eternally begotten of the Father' is the orthodox boundary.
entries.notes: Adoptionism (Son became Son at baptism/incarnation) is heretical; some evangelicals (e.g., MacArthur formerly) flirted with incarnational sonship but later corrected.

entries.statement: Christ is one person in two natures — fully divine and fully human — without confusion, change, division, or separation.
entries.position_rationale: The Chalcedonian Definition codifies the biblical witness; the natures are united in the one person of the Son without mixing or separating.
entries.notes: Excludes Nestorianism (two persons), Eutychianism/monophysitism (mixed nature), Apollinarianism (incomplete humanity).

entries.statement: Christ died as a substitute, bearing the penalty for the sins of His people in their place.
entries.position_rationale: Isaiah 53, Romans 3:25, 2 Corinthians 5:21, and 1 Peter 2:24 ground penal substitution; the heart of the Brethren-tradition gospel.
entries.notes: Denial of penal substitution (e.g., Christus Victor only, moral influence only, denial of wrath-bearing) functionally guts the gospel.

entries.statement: Jesus Christ was conceived by the Holy Spirit and born of the virgin Mary.
entries.position_rationale: Matthew 1:18-25 and Luke 1:26-35 explicitly teach virginal conception; foundational to incarnation and a historic creedal essential.
entries.notes: Liberal Protestantism often denies; this is one of the fundamentals of the fundamentalist-modernist controversy.

entries.statement: The risen Christ is exalted at the right hand of the Father, reigning and interceding for His people.
entries.position_rationale: Hebrews 1:3, Hebrews 7:25, and Romans 8:34 ground Christ's heavenly session and intercession.

entries.statement: Kenoticism — the view that Christ surrendered or gave up divine attributes in the incarnation — is to be rejected.
entries.position_rationale: Philippians 2:6-7's 'emptying' refers to taking the form of a servant, not to losing deity; Colossians 2:9 affirms the fullness of deity dwells in Christ bodily even now.
entries.notes: Functional kenoticism (Christ used divine attributes voluntarily) can be acceptable; ontological kenoticism is heterodox.

entries.statement: Christ possesses two wills — divine and human — corresponding to His two natures, working in harmony.
entries.position_rationale: Gethsemane (Luke 22:42, 'not my will but thine') displays a real human will distinct from the divine will yet submitted; affirmed at the Sixth Ecumenical Council.
entries.notes: Monothelitism was condemned but most modern evangelicals don't engage this question; not a primary divider.

entries.statement: The Holy Spirit is fully and truly God, co-equal and co-eternal with the Father and the Son.
entries.position_rationale: Acts 5:3-4 (lying to the Spirit = lying to God), 1 Corinthians 3:16, and the baptismal formula (Matt 28:19) ground the Spirit's deity.
entries.notes: Watchtower denies Spirit's deity and personhood — a primary cult marker.

entries.statement: The Holy Spirit indwells every true believer permanently from the moment of regeneration.
entries.position_rationale: Romans 8:9 ('if anyone does not have the Spirit of Christ, he does not belong to him') makes indwelling the mark of every Christian; John 14:16-17 promises permanence.
entries.notes: Some sacramental traditions teach Spirit-reception only via baptism/confirmation by clergy — Brethren reject this strongly.

entries.statement: The Holy Spirit is a personal being — possessing intellect, will, and emotion — not an impersonal force or energy.
entries.position_rationale: John 14-16 attributes teaching, willing, grieving, and personal pronouns to the Spirit; Ephesians 4:30 ('grieve not the Spirit') requires personhood.
entries.notes: Watchtower's 'active force' doctrine fails this directly.

entries.statement: The Holy Spirit is the agent of regeneration, granting new birth to those dead in sin.
entries.position_rationale: John 3:5-8 and Titus 3:5 ground regeneration as the Spirit's work; without the Spirit's quickening, no one can come to Christ.

entries.statement: Blasphemy against the Holy Spirit — final, hardened rejection of His witness to Christ — is unforgivable.
entries.position_rationale: Mark 3:28-30 and Matthew 12:31-32 teach the unpardonable sin in the context of attributing Christ's Spirit-empowered works to Satan; Brethren tradition treats this seriously but pastorally, with assurance that those who fear they have committed it likely have not.
entries.notes: Pastoral application matters more than precise definition — anxious sinners seeking forgiveness have not committed this sin.

entries.statement: Spirit baptism (being baptized into the body of Christ by the Spirit) occurs at conversion for every believer, not as a subsequent second blessing.
entries.position_rationale: 1 Corinthians 12:13 says 'we were all baptized by one Spirit into one body' — universal to believers, not a post-conversion experience; Brethren tradition holds this against second-blessing Pentecostalism.
entries.notes: Pentecostal/charismatic 'second-blessing' Spirit-baptism (often with tongues as initial evidence) is the divide. Cessationism details belong in slot 5.

entries.statement: The Holy Spirit illumines Scripture to believers, enabling spiritual understanding of God's word.
entries.position_rationale: 1 Corinthians 2:10-14 and 1 John 2:27 ground illumination; the Spirit does not give new revelation but opens the believer's mind to the inscripturated word.
entries.notes: Crucial distinction: illumination opens what is written; it does not add new revelation that binds conscience.

entries.statement: The Holy Spirit seals every believer as God's possession, guaranteeing their final inheritance.
entries.position_rationale: Ephesians 1:13-14 and 4:30 explicitly describe the Spirit as the seal and down-payment; Brethren tradition affirms this as ground of eternal security.
entries.notes: Connected to eternal security — see slot 3 soteriology for fuller treatment.

entries.statement: The Holy Spirit eternally proceeds from the Father (and from the Son, per the Western tradition).
entries.position_rationale: John 15:26 and the Western filioque tradition; Brethren tradition typically follows the Western reading without making it a dividing line with Eastern Orthodoxy.
entries.notes: Filioque is the major East-West divide; not church-dividing for me but technically an open question.

entries.statement: Adam and Eve were real historical persons, the first human pair, from whom all humanity descends; they were specially created and not the product of unguided evolution.
entries.position_rationale: Required by Pauline federal-headship theology (Rom 5; 1 Cor 15); denial collapses the parallel between First and Last Adam and undermines original sin.
entries.notes: Mythologizing Adam (as in some theistic-evolution frameworks) erodes the gospel's historical scaffolding. Age of earth is separate question.

entries.statement: God created humanity male and female as a fixed binary rooted in creation; biological sex and gender identity are coherent and not subject to self-determined redefinition.
entries.position_rationale: Genesis 1:27 grounds gender in creation, reaffirmed by Christ (Matt 19:4); denial undermines marriage, sexuality, and the Creator-creature distinction.
entries.notes: Pastoral compassion required toward those with dysphoria; doctrinal clarity not negotiable. Increasingly a status confessionis issue in modern West.

entries.statement: All human beings are created in the image of God (imago Dei), bearing inherent dignity, moral agency, rationality, and capacity for relationship with God, irrespective of age, ability, ethnicity, or stage of development.
entries.position_rationale: Foundational to Genesis 1 anthropology and to a coherent Christian ethic of life, dignity, and personhood; affirmed across Brethren tradition and historic Protestant orthodoxy.
entries.notes: Denial typically signals deeper anthropological distortion (e.g., racism, eugenics, transhumanism); marred by sin but not erased.

entries.statement: Human beings are a unity of material body and immaterial soul/spirit; whether constitution is dichotomous (body+soul) or trichotomous (body+soul+spirit) is a debated but in-house question.
entries.position_rationale: Brethren tradition has historically leaned trichotomous (1 Thess 5:23; Heb 4:12), though Reformed orthodoxy is dichotomous; either view preserves immaterial-soul reality against physicalism.
entries.notes: What matters is rejection of physicalism/materialism and affirmation of conscious intermediate state, not which schema.

entries.statement: The Fall was a real historical event in which Adam, as covenant head, disobeyed God, plunging humanity and the created order into sin, death, and corruption.
entries.position_rationale: The historicity of the Fall is necessary for a coherent doctrine of redemption; without a real Fall, there is no real need for a real Redeemer.

entries.statement: Fallen humans, apart from the Spirit's prior work, are unable to please God, repent, or believe savingly; the natural man cannot receive the things of the Spirit.
entries.position_rationale: Direct corollary of total depravity; affirmed across Reformation traditions including Brethren formation, even where prevenient grace (rather than effectual call) is the mechanism.

entries.statement: All human beings are born with a sinful nature inherited from Adam, sharing in his guilt and corruption, and are by nature objects of divine wrath.
entries.position_rationale: Affirmed across Brethren tradition, Reformation confessions, and Augustinian consensus; required by Romans 5 and Ephesians 2:1-3.
entries.notes: Denial trends toward Pelagianism and ultimately works-salvation; tightly linked to need for substitutionary atonement.

entries.statement: Sin is any want of conformity to, or transgression of, the law of God — in thought, word, deed, and disposition — and is fundamentally an offense against God's holy character, not merely against neighbour.
entries.position_rationale: 1 John 3:4 defines sin as lawlessness; without an objective divine standard, sin becomes therapeutic dysfunction and the cross becomes incoherent.
entries.notes: Modern therapeutic redefinitions of sin as 'brokenness' alone are insufficient — sin is also guilt before God.

entries.statement: Sin has corrupted every faculty of fallen man — mind, will, affections, body — such that no part is untouched and unaided man cannot save himself or initiate saving faith.
entries.position_rationale: Brethren tradition affirms total depravity (extent, not degree) even while typically rejecting other points of high Calvinism; necessary against Pelagian/semi-Pelagian optimism about human nature.
entries.notes: Total depravity does not mean utter wickedness in every act; it means no part is untouched and salvation is wholly of grace.

entries.statement: Conscience is a divinely-given internal witness to God's law that, though damaged by sin, accuses or excuses moral agents and renders all without excuse before God.
entries.position_rationale: Romans 2 grounds general accountability of all humanity; conscience is real but not infallible — it must be informed and re-formed by Scripture.
entries.notes: Conscience can be seared (1 Tim 4:2) and must not become a substitute for revealed Scripture.

entries.statement: Adam acted as the covenant/federal head of humanity, so that his sin and its consequences are imputed to all his natural descendants, paralleling Christ as federal head of the redeemed.
entries.position_rationale: Romans 5 and 1 Corinthians 15 require a federal-representative reading; without it, the imputed-righteousness side of justification also collapses.
entries.notes: Some Brethren prefer a more 'seminal' or 'realistic' framing over federalism but arrive at the same imputation result.

entries.statement: Idolatry — exchanging the glory of the Creator for created things — is the root of all sin; every transgression is a derivative form of misplaced worship.
entries.position_rationale: Romans 1 traces the moral collapse to idolatrous exchange; useful for diagnostic preaching and pastoral counselling.

entries.statement: At Christ's return, believers will be raised bodily, transformed, and glorified — the final consummation of salvation in resurrection bodies free from sin and decay.
entries.position_rationale: The 'already-not yet' completion of salvation; Brethren tradition has historically been strong on bodily resurrection and final glorification.

entries.statement: In justification, the active and passive righteousness of Christ is imputed (reckoned, credited) to the believer, so that the believer stands clothed in Christ's perfect obedience before God.
entries.position_rationale: The 'great exchange' (2 Cor 5:21) is the heart of the gospel; both sides — sin imputed to Christ, righteousness imputed to believer — must be held.
entries.notes: Some New Perspective on Paul advocates deny imputation specifically; this remains a serious doctrinal error.

entries.statement: Justification is a forensic, declarative act of God in which He pardons sinners and accounts them righteous in His sight — not on the basis of any inherent change, but solely on account of Christ's imputed righteousness received by faith.
entries.position_rationale: Forensic ('declared' not 'made') justification is the Reformation insight against Roman infusion; denial moves toward sacramental works-salvation.
entries.notes: Distinct from sanctification: justification is once-for-all, declarative; sanctification is progressive, transformative.

entries.statement: Pelagianism — the teaching that humans are born morally neutral, unaffected by Adam's sin, and able to choose God and obey His law by natural ability — is a denied heresy.
entries.position_rationale: Condemned at Council of Carthage (418), Council of Ephesus (431); incompatible with Romans 5, Ephesians 2, and the necessity of the cross.
entries.notes: Note: 'denied' here means I deny Pelagianism. Field semantics: personal_position=deny means I reject the named error. Heretical not cult-grade because not always salvation-collapsing in every form, but typically is.

entries.statement: The atonement is penal in nature: Christ bore the just punishment and divine wrath that sinners deserved, satisfying God's justice and propitiating His wrath.
entries.position_rationale: Propitiation language (hilasmos/hilasterion) requires a wrath-bearing substitute; rejection of penal substitution typically reduces the cross to example or moral influence.
entries.notes: Other models (Christus Victor, recapitulation) are valid complements but not substitutes; PSA must remain central.

entries.statement: Religious pluralism (all paths lead to God) and Christian inclusivism (people may be saved through other religions because of Christ without explicit faith) are denied; explicit faith in the historical Jesus Christ is necessary for salvation.
entries.position_rationale: John 14:6, Acts 4:12, Rom 10:13-17 require named-Christ faith; pluralism collapses the cross's necessity; inclusivism (Rahner-style 'anonymous Christians') softens but ultimately undermines missionary urgency.
entries.notes: WCF 10.4 narrowly leaves room for elect-infants/incapable; that is not pluralism. The unevangelized adult question is hard but the answer is missionary urgency, not pluralism.

entries.statement: The prosperity gospel — that faith in Christ guarantees material wealth, physical health, and earthly success, often via 'sowing' financial seed — is a denied false gospel.
entries.position_rationale: Distorts the cross, exploits the vulnerable, and replaces the kingdom with consumerism; treats God as cosmic vending-machine. Especially destructive in Indian/Global-South contexts.
entries.notes: Soft prosperity-adjacent teaching ('your best life now') is a milder form but on the same trajectory; treat as evangelism field.

entries.statement: Repentance — a Spirit-given change of mind toward sin and toward God, issuing in turning from sin to Christ — is integral to saving conversion, never separable from saving faith.
entries.position_rationale: Christ's first preaching was 'repent and believe' (Mark 1:15); a 'gospel' without repentance is not the biblical gospel.
entries.notes: Disputes about 'how much repentance is required' (Lordship vs. Free Grace) are in-house; total absence of repentance is not.

entries.statement: Justification is by faith alone (sola fide) — faith being the empty hand that receives Christ's imputed righteousness; works are the necessary fruit, never the ground, of justification.
entries.position_rationale: Articulus stantis et cadentis ecclesiae per the Reformers; central in Brethren tradition; explicitly denied by Trent and modern works-systems.
entries.notes: James 2 harmonizes: faith without works is dead, but works are vindicating evidence, not justifying ground.

entries.statement: Salvation is by grace alone (sola gratia) — wholly God's unmerited favour, never earned, contributed to, or co-purchased by human effort, ritual, or pedigree.
entries.position_rationale: Cornerstone of biblical and Reformation soteriology; affirmed by Brethren tradition; denial corrupts the gospel itself.
entries.notes: Denial = false gospel (Gal 1:8-9). Cult-grade when grace is replaced by works/sacraments/secret knowledge.

entries.statement: Salvation is in Christ alone (solus Christus); there is no other name under heaven by which men must be saved, no other mediator between God and man.
entries.position_rationale: Direct biblical claim (John 14:6; Acts 4:12; 1 Tim 2:5); denial collapses into pluralism or inclusivism.

entries.statement: Christ died as a substitute in the place of sinners, bearing the penalty due for their sin so that they might be reconciled to God.
entries.position_rationale: Substitutionary logic pervades Scripture from Passover lamb to Isaiah 53 to NT atonement language; central to Brethren preaching.

entries.statement: Works-righteousness — any system in which human merit, sacraments, ritual, law-keeping, or moral effort partly or wholly grounds justification before God — is a denied false gospel.
entries.position_rationale: Galatians 1 anathematizes any other gospel; works-righteousness directly negates sola gratia and sola fide. Cult-grade.
entries.notes: Cult-grade because it inverts the gospel itself. Includes Tridentine Roman, Eastern works-synergy, sectarian legalism, etc.

entries.statement: By adoption, those justified are received into the family of God, granted the privileges, name, and inheritance of sons through union with Christ and indwelling of the Spirit of adoption.
entries.position_rationale: Adoption is the relational summit of soteriology (Rom 8; Gal 4); critical pastoral doctrine for assurance and identity.

entries.statement: Antinomianism — that grace abolishes any binding role for God's moral law in the Christian life, so that obedience is optional or irrelevant — is a denied error.
entries.position_rationale: Romans 6 explicitly condemns 'continue in sin that grace may abound'; the Spirit writes the law on the believer's heart (Jer 31; Heb 8). Free Grace circles sometimes drift here.
entries.notes: Distinct from confessional 'third use of the law' debates; antinomianism proper denies any binding moral norm.

entries.statement: Conversion is the conscious human response to the gospel — repentance and faith — by which the regenerated sinner turns from sin to Christ; both gift of God and act of the believer.
entries.position_rationale: Conversion is the human side of regeneration's divine work; Brethren tradition emphasizes a definite, datable conversion in many testimonies, while not making the moment of decision the saving thing.

entries.statement: Legalism — adding human rules to God's commands as conditions of acceptance, fellowship, or sanctification, or treating obedience as the ground rather than fruit of grace — is a denied error.
entries.position_rationale: Christ's strongest rebukes were against legalists (Matt 23); Brethren tradition has historically been alert to legalism even while sometimes drifting into it culturally (dress codes, separatism rules). Particular pastoral concern.
entries.notes: Diagnostic mismatch this baseline is built to expose: doctrinally-precise churches often legalism-positive, fruit-rich churches often loose. Both errors. The goal is grace + truth + love.

entries.statement: Those truly born again will be kept by the power of God through faith unto final salvation; genuine believers cannot ultimately and finally fall away.
entries.position_rationale: Brethren tradition strongly affirms eternal security (John 10:28-29; Rom 8:38-39; Phil 1:6); apparent apostates are understood as never genuinely regenerate (1 John 2:19).
entries.notes: Wesleyan/classical Arminian denial is a real historic Protestant position; in-house but consequential pastorally. Distinguish from 'carnal Christian' / cheap-grace abuse.

entries.statement: Sanctification is the progressive, Spirit-wrought work of conforming believers to the image of Christ — distinct from justification but inseparable from it; engaged through means of grace and Spirit-empowered effort.
entries.position_rationale: Held by Brethren; opposed both to perfectionism (claiming sinless attainment) and antinomian quietism. Mortify-and-vivify is the apostolic pattern.
entries.notes: Some Brethren historically engaged Keswick / 'higher life' models; classical progressive view is sturdier.

entries.statement: Regeneration (the new birth) is a monergistic work of the Holy Spirit, sovereignly imparting spiritual life to those dead in sin; logically prior to and the cause of saving faith.
entries.position_rationale: John 3 / John 6 / Ezekiel 36 / Eph 2 require God-initiated new birth; even most Arminian Brethren grant prevenient grace must precede genuine response.
entries.notes: Distinct from decisional-regeneration (which inverts the order) — see doc-decisional-regeneration.

entries.statement: Semi-Pelagianism — the teaching that the sinner takes the first step toward God by natural ability, with grace then assisting — is a denied error.
entries.position_rationale: Condemned at Second Council of Orange (529); inverts the order of grace; in practice describes a great deal of folk-evangelical altar-call theology.
entries.notes: Many otherwise-evangelical churches drift into functional semi-Pelagianism without realizing it; useful diagnostic for evaluating church soundness.

entries.statement: Believers may have well-grounded assurance of salvation, founded on the promises of God in Christ, the inward witness of the Spirit, and the evidence of Spirit-wrought fruit.
entries.position_rationale: Triple ground (objective promise, subjective Spirit, evidential fruit) per 1 John; counters both presumption (no fruit) and despair (no promise-grasping).
entries.notes: Sacramental traditions tend to deny present-life assurance; this is a serious pastoral defect even where gospel essentials are nominally affirmed.

entries.statement: Decisional regeneration — the view that salvation is effected by the act of decision (raised hand, walked aisle, repeated prayer) rather than by Spirit-wrought new birth received through faith — is a methodological error to be resisted.
entries.position_rationale: Brethren tradition uses gospel invitations and 'decisions' practically, but with strong reservations against treating the decision-act itself as the saving thing. The line between healthy invitation and decisional regeneration is real and important.
entries.notes: Diagnostic: does the church point converts to Christ + ongoing repentance/faith, or to the date of their decision? The latter produces false assurance epidemics.

entries.statement: God calls His people through the Word and Spirit — outwardly through preaching and inwardly so that the call is effective unto salvation in those who respond by faith.
entries.position_rationale: Whether framed as effectual call (Reformed) or prevenient + responded-to call (Arminian), Brethren affirm Spirit-empowered, Word-mediated calling.

entries.statement: Before the foundation of the world, God chose a people for salvation in Christ; whether election is unconditional (Calvinist) or based on foreseen faith (classical Arminian/Brethren) is a divided question.
entries.position_rationale: Brethren tradition tends Arminian or moderate on election; affirms divine sovereignty and human responsibility without endorsing reprobation as flip-side. Both views hold within historic Protestantism.
entries.notes: The denial of election altogether (Pelagian/open-theist style) is not in-house; that would tier up to convictional.

entries.statement: The atonement is sufficient for all but applied/efficient for the elect (or, in some traditions, genuinely universal in provision); Brethren tradition typically holds general atonement, with sincere universal gospel offer.
entries.position_rationale: Brethren tradition is generally non-Calvinist or 4-point; affirms Christ died for all (1 Tim 2:6; 1 John 2:2) while acknowledging only believers are saved. Particular-redemption Calvinists hold a defensible alternative.
entries.notes: An in-house Protestant debate; both sides preach the same gospel offer to all.

entries.statement: God ordinarily nourishes faith and sanctifies His people through appointed means: the Word read and preached, prayer, the breaking of bread, baptism, and Spirit-empowered Christian fellowship.
entries.position_rationale: Means are means, not magic; Brethren tradition has been particularly strong on Word + Lord's Supper + prayer as ordinary means without sacerdotal mediation.

entries.statement: The 'sinner's prayer' as a evangelistic tool is permissible if it expresses genuine repentance and faith, but is dangerous when treated as a saving formula or basis for assurance apart from ongoing fruit.
entries.position_rationale: Brethren practice generally embraces gospel-prayers but resists treating the prayer itself as salvific; assurance must rest on Christ + Spirit + fruit, not on a prior verbal transaction.
entries.notes: Method, not doctrine. The doctrine behind it (regeneration, faith, repentance) is what must be evaluated.

entries.statement: Christ alone is the Head of the Church; no human being holds that office or shares that authority.
entries.position_rationale: Scripture is unambiguous: Christ is sole Head. Any human claiming to be vicar, prophet, or sole conduit of revelation usurps Christ's office — this is the classic cult-leader pattern.
entries.notes: The one cult-marker in this slot. If a group exalts a human leader to a position only Christ holds (sole revelator, ultimate authority over conscience, vicar of Christ on earth in the strong sense), treat as cult-grade aberration.

entries.statement: The Church is the body of Christ, a spiritual organism composed of all true believers from Pentecost to the rapture, united to Christ by the indwelling Holy Spirit.
entries.position_rationale: Pauline ecclesiology presents the Church as Christ's body and bride, not a human institution. Brethren tradition strongly emphasizes the spiritual, organic nature of the Church over institutional forms.
confessional_anchors: Nicene Creed (one holy catholic apostolic Church)
entries.notes: Foundational ecclesiology. Denial typically signals serious doctrinal aberration (cult, deeply unorthodox group).

entries.statement: Each local assembly is autonomous under Christ's headship, governed by its own elders, not subordinate to a denominational hierarchy or external bishop.
entries.position_rationale: Brethren and broader free-church distinctive. NT churches are seen consulting one another (Acts 15) but not subjected to a hierarchy.
entries.notes: Episcopal and presbyterian polities are widely held by orthodox Christians. User's tradition affirms congregational/independent polity. Membership commitment is for autonomous-assembly churches; visiting episcopal/presbyterian churches is fine.

entries.statement: A true visible church is marked by: (1) the Word of God rightly preached, (2) the ordinances rightly administered, (3) church discipline rightly exercised.
entries.position_rationale: These three Reformation marks are the classic Protestant test for church validity. A gathering missing all three is not a true church regardless of name.
entries.notes: Practical evaluation grid for London church search. A group with strong programs but no expository preaching, careless ordinances, or zero discipline fails the marks test.

entries.statement: Scripture knows no two-tier clergy/laity distinction; all believers are saints and ministers, with elders functioning as servant-overseers, not a priestly class.
entries.position_rationale: Brethren distinctive flowing from priesthood-of-all-believers. NT terminology for ministers (servants, overseers, shepherds) is functional, not sacerdotal. The clergy/laity wall is a post-apostolic development.
confessional_anchors: Brethren tradition (no formal confession; embodied in assembly practice)
entries.notes: User position formed in Brethren tradition. Visiting and participating in clergy-led churches is fine for evaluation, but membership requires alignment on plurality and lay-ministry principles. Most evangelical churches have a soft clergy/laity practice without strong sacerdotal claims.

entries.statement: The Church is one (united in Christ), holy (set apart, indwelt by the Spirit), catholic (universal across time and place), and apostolic (built on the apostles' teaching, not on apostolic succession of office).
entries.position_rationale: Affirm the Nicene marks understood Protestantly: apostolic = continuity with apostolic doctrine, not unbroken episcopal succession.
entries.notes: Affirm the Nicene marks; reject Roman/Orthodox interpretation of 'apostolic' as unbroken episcopal succession. Doctrinal continuity, not institutional pedigree, is the real apostolicity.

entries.statement: Every believer is a priest before God through Christ; there is no mediatorial priesthood between Christ and the believer beyond Christ Himself.
entries.position_rationale: Reformation principle, strongly held in Brethren tradition. Direct access to God through Christ alone; no sacerdotal class is needed to mediate grace.
entries.notes: Rome and Eastern Orthodoxy hold a sacerdotal priesthood; this is a confessional Protestant divide. Brethren tradition is the strongest expression of this principle (no clergy/laity).

entries.statement: The Church exists in two senses: universal (all believers in all times and places) and local (particular gatherings of believers in covenanted fellowship).
entries.position_rationale: Both senses are clearly taught in Scripture. Universal Church language (Eph 1, Heb 12) and local-assembly language (the church at Corinth, Ephesus, etc.) are both biblical.
entries.notes: Some hyper-local groups deny the universal Church concept — that's a Brethren-adjacent error. Most orthodox traditions hold both.

entries.statement: Baptism does not regenerate, save, or wash away sin in itself (ex opere operato); it is the outward sign of an inward reality already accomplished by grace through faith.
entries.position_rationale: Salvation is by grace through faith alone (Eph 2:8-9). Sacraments are signs and seals, not the cause of grace. Treating water as the saving instrument confuses sign and thing-signified.
entries.notes: Heterodox-bordering-heretical when held strongly (Rome, some Lutheran formulations, Church of Christ). Treating baptism as the saving instrument compromises sola fide. Distinct from sacramental realism (Lutheran 'effectual sign').

entries.statement: The Lord's Supper is primarily a memorial of Christ's once-for-all atoning death, accompanied by spiritual communion with the risen Christ by faith. It is not a re-sacrifice and the elements do not change in substance.
entries.position_rationale: 'Do this in remembrance of me' (Luke 22:19; 1 Cor 11:24-26). Brethren tradition holds a memorial view with strong spiritual presence overtones — feeding on Christ by faith, not on transformed elements.
entries.notes: User position is memorial + spiritual presence (Calvinist-Brethren synthesis). Lutheran consubstantiation and Calvin's spiritual-feeding view are within tolerable orthodoxy. Roman transubstantiation is rejected.

entries.statement: Infant baptism (paedobaptism) lacks New Testament warrant; the covenant-of-grace argument from circumcision is theologically inadequate to override the consistent NT pattern of faith preceding baptism.
entries.position_rationale: Every recorded NT baptism follows profession of faith. Paedobaptism is inferred from covenant theology, not commanded. Brethren and Baptist traditions reject it.
entries.notes: Massive orthodox tradition holds paedobaptism (Reformed, Anglican, Lutheran, Presbyterian, Methodist, Rome, Eastern Orthodoxy). User denies but recognises paedobaptists as fellow Christians. Not break-fellowship; is membership-deciding.

entries.statement: Transubstantiation — the Roman doctrine that the substance of the bread and wine is changed into the substance of Christ's body and blood while only the accidents remain — is unbiblical and philosophically untenable.
entries.position_rationale: Christ's bodily ascension means his physical body is at the Father's right hand. The Mass as ongoing propitiatory sacrifice undermines Hebrews' once-for-all completed atonement. The Aristotelian substance/accident framework is imposed on Scripture.
entries.notes: Reformers were martyred over this. The Mass-as-sacrifice is gospel-adjacent and serious. Reject definitively but classify Rome as heterodox Christianity, not cult.

entries.statement: Christ instituted two ordinances for the Church: baptism and the Lord's Supper. They are signs and seals, not means of saving grace ex opere operato.
entries.position_rationale: Only baptism and the Supper are explicitly commanded by Christ for ongoing church practice. Brethren prefer 'ordinance' (commanded) over 'sacrament' (mystery) to underline non-mystical nature.
entries.notes: Rome and Eastern Orthodoxy hold seven sacraments; Protestants generally hold two. Brethren use 'ordinance' to distance from Roman sacramentology.

entries.statement: Baptism is for professing believers only, administered by full immersion as a public confession of faith and identification with Christ's death, burial, and resurrection.
entries.position_rationale: NT pattern is consistent: faith precedes baptism. The mode (immersion) symbolises burial and resurrection (Rom 6). Brethren distinctive that aligns with broader Baptist tradition.
entries.notes: Membership-deciding for user. Will visit/participate at paedobaptist churches (Reformed, Anglican, Lutheran) but cannot formally join a church that requires endorsement of infant baptism. Reasonable disagreement among orthodox Christians; not break-fellowship-level.

entries.statement: Consubstantiation (the Lutheran 'in, with, and under' the elements) preserves real-presence language without transubstantiation but introduces ubiquity claims about Christ's body that strain Christology.
entries.position_rationale: Lutheran view is closer to orthodoxy than Rome's, but the doctrine of bodily ubiquity is problematic. User holds memorial-plus-spiritual-presence view.
entries.notes: Confessional Lutheran position. User considers it within orthodoxy and would visit/participate, but holds memorial view personally.

entries.statement: The Roman Catholic seven-sacrament system (baptism, confirmation, eucharist, penance, anointing of the sick, holy orders, matrimony) lacks NT warrant for treating five of those as Christ-instituted ordinances of the Church.
entries.position_rationale: Only baptism and the Supper are dominically instituted ordinances. The other five are good practices or natural institutions but not sacraments in the same sense.
entries.notes: Rome's sacramental system is bound up with sacerdotalism, ex opere operato grace, and Roman gospel issues. User would not visit/participate in Roman Mass as worship. Catholic individuals are evangelism candidates depending on what gospel they have actually heard.

entries.statement: Foot-washing (John 13) is a beautiful pattern of servant love but was not instituted by Christ as an ongoing ordinance for the Church alongside baptism and the Supper.
entries.position_rationale: Christ's command 'do as I have done' teaches the principle of mutual servanthood, not a literal ritual ordinance. The early church did not treat it as parallel to baptism and the Supper.
entries.notes: Anabaptist and some Pentecostal traditions practice foot-washing as a third ordinance. Adiaphora-adjacent — not a sticking point either way.

entries.statement: Communion should be open to all believers in Christ who are walking in fellowship with the Lord and not under church discipline, regardless of denominational affiliation or specific baptismal mode.
entries.position_rationale: The Table is the Lord's, not the local assembly's. Self-examination (1 Cor 11) is the biblical guard. Open Brethren tradition (vs. Exclusive) holds this; user aligns with open practice.
entries.notes: Closed/close communion (Exclusive Brethren, some Reformed Baptists) is held by sincere brothers. User prefers open practice; not membership-breaking either way unless taken to sectarian extremes.

entries.statement: The Lord's Supper (breaking of bread) is observed weekly on the Lord's Day as the central act of corporate worship.
entries.position_rationale: Acts 20:7 indicates weekly gathering specifically to break bread. The early church practice was weekly. Brethren distinctive recovers this against monthly/quarterly Protestant practice.
entries.notes: Beautiful Brethren practice but not membership-breaking. A doctrinally sound church with monthly Supper is fully acceptable. User would prefer weekly but not require it.

entries.statement: Apostolic succession as an unbroken episcopal line conferring valid ministry, sacraments, and ecclesiastical authority is unbiblical; true apostolic continuity is doctrinal fidelity to apostolic teaching.
entries.position_rationale: NT knows no apostolic-succession claim. Apostolicity is doctrinal (Eph 2:20, Jude 3), not institutional. Rome's claim of unbroken succession from Peter is historically untenable and theologically misplaced.
entries.notes: Held by Rome, Eastern Orthodoxy, high-church Anglicans. Heterodox-not-cult. Rome is a heterodox-Christian tradition with serious gospel concerns, not a cult. Affirming successionists who hold orthodox christology and trinitarianism are still in the Christian family broadly understood.

entries.statement: Episcopal polity — bishops with authority over multiple local churches as a distinct tier above presbyters — lacks NT warrant; the NT uses 'bishop' (overseer) and 'elder' interchangeably for the same office.
entries.position_rationale: Acts 20:17/28 and Titus 1:5/7 show episkopos and presbuteros as one office. The mono-episcopate is a second-century development.
entries.notes: Anglican, Roman, Eastern Orthodox, Methodist hold episcopal polity. Many godly believers and orthodox doctrine within these traditions. User holds congregational/independent view; visiting Anglican worship fine.

entries.statement: Papal supremacy and infallibility — the Bishop of Rome as head of the universal Church and infallible in faith and morals when speaking ex cathedra — has no biblical or early-church warrant.
entries.position_rationale: Christ alone is Head of the Church (Eph 1:22). Peter received no transferable primacy; the rock of Matt 16 is Peter's confession, not his office. Papal infallibility (1870) is a recent dogma without ancient consensus.
entries.notes: Reformers were martyred over this. The Pope is a man, not Christ's vicar in the strong sense. Distinct from cult-leader claims because the office, not a single individual, is exalted; still a serious aberration. Rome remains Christian-heterodox, not cult.

entries.statement: Each local assembly should be led by a plurality of qualified elders (overseers/shepherds), not a single senior pastor; elders rule and shepherd jointly.
entries.position_rationale: NT consistently uses plural elders for each local church (Acts 14:23; 20:17; Titus 1:5; Phil 1:1). Brethren distinctive that protects against autocracy and lone-pastor burnout. Strong scriptural pattern.
entries.notes: Membership-deciding for user. Many sound evangelical churches operate on senior-pastor model — visit and participate fine. Formal membership requires plurality of elders. London search: prioritise plural-elder churches.

entries.statement: The single-pastor / senior-pastor-as-CEO model lacks NT warrant; concentrating authority and teaching ministry in one man departs from the apostolic plurality pattern and tends toward personality-driven dysfunction.
entries.position_rationale: Concentration of authority creates accountability gaps, celebrity-pastor culture, and church-splits when the man falls. Plurality is biblical and prudential.
entries.notes: Most evangelical churches in London are senior-pastor model. User can visit/participate; membership requires functional plurality. Watch for: real plurality vs. nominal elder boards rubber-stamping a CEO-pastor.

entries.statement: The office of elder/overseer is reserved for qualified men; women are not to hold the eldership/teaching-pastor office over mixed adult assemblies.
entries.position_rationale: 1 Tim 2:11-15 and 1 Tim 3:1-7 (qualifications include 'husband of one wife') establish male eldership. Brethren tradition consistently complementarian.
entries.notes: Egalitarian view is widely held by orthodox Christians today, particularly in mainline and many evangelical churches. User holds complementarian view firmly but recognises sincere disagreement. Membership-deciding; fellowship/visiting fine. Distinguish from full-blown ordination of women bishops in liberal traditions tied to broader gospel issues.

entries.statement: Women should not exercise authoritative teaching ministry over mixed adult assemblies in the gathered church (sermon, doctrinal instruction); women teaching women, children, or in informal contexts is not in view.
entries.position_rationale: 1 Tim 2:12 specifically restricts authoritative teaching over men in the gathered assembly. Titus 2 commands women to teach women. The complementary pattern affirms women's gifting in non-authoritative contexts.
entries.notes: Complementarian distinction. Priscilla teaching Apollos (privately, with husband) is the model for informal exhortation. Women preaching from the pulpit to mixed adults is not user's practice; visiting such churches fine but membership unlikely.

entries.statement: Congregational polity — final earthly authority resting in the gathered congregation under Christ, with elders leading and the assembly affirming — best preserves NT autonomy and accountability.
entries.position_rationale: NT shows the assembly involved in major decisions (Acts 6, Acts 15:22, 1 Cor 5, 2 Cor 2:6). Elders lead but do not supplant the body's responsibility.
entries.notes: User affirms congregational polity in elder-led form. Pure democracy without elder leadership tends to dysfunction; pure elder rule without congregational consent tends to autocracy. Balance is biblical.

entries.statement: Deacons are a NT office of servant-ministers handling practical care and administration in the local assembly, distinct from the teaching/oversight role of elders.
entries.position_rationale: Acts 6 (proto-deacon pattern) and 1 Tim 3:8-13 establish a distinct serving office. Deacons free elders for prayer and the Word.
entries.notes: Some Brethren assemblies have informal serving brothers without formal deacon titles — same function, different label. Acceptable variation.

entries.statement: Formal ordination as conferral of sacerdotal status or unique ministerial grace is rejected; recognition of elders/deacons by the local assembly with prayer and laying on of hands is biblical, but creates no clergy class.
entries.position_rationale: Brethren distinctive. NT laying-on-of-hands recognises gifts and commissions for service, not transfer of sacramental power. Ordination has often hardened into a clergy/laity wall.
entries.notes: User accepts assembly recognition of elders. Rejects ordination-as-status (Reverend titles, pulpit rights tied to credentials). Many evangelical churches have moderate ordination practice that is acceptable.

entries.statement: Presbyterian polity — graded courts (session, presbytery, general assembly) of teaching and ruling elders — has biblical merit (Acts 15) but tends toward over-institutionalisation and erodes local-assembly autonomy.
entries.position_rationale: Acts 15 supports inter-church consultation, not subordination of the local assembly to a higher court. User affirms autonomy of the local assembly.
entries.notes: Strong Reformed tradition (PCA, OPC, Free Church of Scotland, etc.). Doctrinally rich; user respects but holds independent polity. Visiting/participating fine; membership unlikely.

entries.statement: Women may serve in significant diaconal, hospitality, mercy, evangelism, women's ministry, children's ministry, missionary, and helping roles; gifting and calling are not gender-restricted in non-authoritative contexts.
entries.position_rationale: Phoebe (Rom 16:1, diakonos), Priscilla, Lydia, Junia, the women at the resurrection — Scripture honours women in extensive ministry. Complementarianism is not the suppression of women's ministry, only restriction of the elder office and authoritative teaching of mixed adults.
entries.notes: Some hyper-restrictive Brethren circles minimise women's ministry beyond Scripture's restrictions — that is its own error. User affirms full women's ministry within complementarian boundaries.

entries.statement: Both bivocational eldership (tentmaking) and full-time supported ministry are biblically valid; neither is the only legitimate form, and Brethren tradition has favoured bivocational/lay eldership.
entries.position_rationale: Paul tentmaking (Acts 18) and 1 Tim 5:17-18 (elders worthy of double honour) both apply. Brethren preference for lay eldership protects against professionalisation but full-time support is also biblical.
entries.notes: User formed in lay-eldership tradition; affirms paid ministry as valid. Watch for: prosperity-style or celebrity-pastor pay structures, which signal a deeper problem.

entries.statement: Formal seminary or theological-college credentials are not biblically required for elder qualification; the NT criteria are character, doctrinal soundness, and ability to teach (1 Tim 3, Titus 1).
entries.position_rationale: Brethren distinctive. NT criteria are character + ability + sound doctrine, not academic credentials. Seminary is helpful but not normative; many faithful elders have been bivocational laypeople with deep self-study.
entries.notes: Confessional Presbyterian and Baptist traditions often require seminary; user holds it as wise but not necessary. Not membership-breaking.

entries.statement: Small to mid-sized assemblies (sub-Dunbar) where members are known by name and elders can shepherd individually are preferable to mega-church structures.
entries.position_rationale: Shepherding metaphor presumes the shepherd knows the sheep (John 10:14). Brethren history of household-sized assemblies. Mega-churches struggle with anonymity and pastoral oversight, though not inherently invalid.
entries.notes: Mega-church model can work if real small-group shepherding is in place. User preference for smaller assembly does not exclude larger churches as members; assess on shepherding reality, not size alone.

entries.statement: Church discipline follows the Matthew 18 progression — private confrontation, small-group confrontation, assembly confrontation, removal — and is exercised in love for repentance and restoration.
entries.position_rationale: Direct dominical command. A church that never disciplines lacks one of the three Reformation marks. Discipline preserves the assembly's purity and witness, and aims at sinner's restoration.
entries.notes: Watch for both extremes: (a) churches that never discipline (false love) and (b) churches that discipline harshly without restoration aim (legalism). Both are dysfunction. The user's tradition has had both forms — discernment required.

entries.statement: Excommunication (removal from fellowship and the Lord's Table) is the final step of the Matthew 18 process for unrepentant sin, intended to provoke repentance and protect the assembly.
entries.position_rationale: 1 Cor 5 and 2 Thess 3 model the practice. The aim is restoration (1 Cor 5:5, 'that the spirit may be saved'). Treating the unrepentant as 'a Gentile and tax collector' (Matt 18:17) does not mean abandonment but reset of relational status.
entries.notes: Some traditions have abused excommunication (sectarian shunning, family-tearing). Open Brethren historically practiced it more carefully than Exclusive branches. Healthy form is loving and restoration-aimed.

entries.statement: When a disciplined member shows credible repentance, the assembly should restore them publicly, forgiving and reaffirming love.
entries.position_rationale: 2 Cor 2:5-11 commands forgiveness and reaffirmation lest the disciplined one be 'overwhelmed by excessive sorrow' and Satan gain advantage. Restoration is the aim of discipline.
entries.notes: Sectarian groups that refuse restoration even after repentance are functioning legalistically. A church that disciplines without ever restoring has misunderstood the process.

entries.statement: Formal church membership — public covenanted commitment between believer and local assembly under elder oversight — is biblically warranted and prudentially essential for accountability and discipline.
entries.position_rationale: Hebrews 13:17, 1 Cor 5 (the assembly disciplines 'the one within'), and the assumption of identifiable membership throughout the NT. Brethren practice varies but most Open Brethren assemblies have functional 'in fellowship' status equivalent to membership.
entries.notes: Some Open Brethren assemblies have informal 'in fellowship' rather than rolls — functionally equivalent. Reject pure consumer-Christianity that refuses any commitment to a local body.

entries.statement: Women are not to take up authoritative speaking ministry (sermon, doctrinal teaching, leading the assembly) in the gathered mixed assembly (1 Cor 14:34-35; 1 Tim 2:11-12); women may pray, sing, prophesy in non-authoritative contexts, share testimony, and minister widely otherwise.
entries.position_rationale: Pauline command tied to creation order, not just culture (1 Tim 2:13-14 grounds it pre-fall). Brethren tradition consistently observes this. Distinct from heavy-handed silencing; women sing, pray, etc. — the restriction is on authoritative speaking that exercises office.
entries.notes: Note exegetical tension: 1 Cor 11:5 mentions women praying/prophesying; 1 Cor 14:34 commands silence. Best harmonisation: 1 Cor 14 restricts authoritative judging of prophecy and disruptive question-asking, not all speaking. User's complementarian position is membership-relevant; visiting egalitarian churches fine.

entries.statement: Head coverings for women during prayer and prophecy in the gathered assembly (1 Cor 11) are practiced in Brethren tradition; the principle (creation-order distinction) is permanent, the form (literal covering) is a culturally embodied expression.
entries.position_rationale: 1 Cor 11:2-16 connects head covering to creation order and angelic witness, suggesting trans-cultural significance. User's tradition practices it. Brethren practice varies by assembly today; many no longer require it.
entries.notes: User affirms practice but recognises legitimate exegetical disagreement (some take Paul as describing a permanent creation principle; others see hair-as-covering or culturally bound). Not membership-breaking. Most evangelical churches do not practice it; user comfortable visiting/joining either way.

entries.statement: Both liturgical (set forms) and spontaneous (Spirit-led) worship are biblically defensible; the regulative principle requires that whatever is done in worship has scriptural warrant, but the form-spectrum is wide.
entries.position_rationale: Scripture mandates elements (Word, prayer, singing, ordinances) but the form is not strictly prescribed. Brethren preference for spontaneity should not become a new legalism against liturgy.
entries.notes: User open to both; prefers Spirit-led spontaneity from formation. Liturgical churches (Anglican, Reformed) have rich worship and biblical content. Avoid extreme charismatic chaos and dead formalism alike.

entries.statement: An open worship/open ministry meeting (any qualified brother may rise as the Spirit leads to read, pray, give thanks, give a word, suggest a hymn) recovers NT participatory worship (1 Cor 14) and is a treasured Brethren distinctive.
entries.position_rationale: 1 Cor 14:26 ('when you come together, each one has a hymn, a lesson...') describes participatory worship rather than monologue service. Brethren breaking-of-bread and ministry meetings preserve this. Beautiful when functioning healthily.
entries.notes: Cherished but not membership-breaking. Most Protestant churches use scheduled liturgy or pulpit-led worship; user can fellowship there. London search: open ministry meeting is rare; weigh against other factors.

entries.statement: Ecumenical or fellowship union with non-Trinitarian groups (Mormonism, Jehovah's Witnesses, Oneness Pentecostalism, Unitarianism) is rejected; these groups are not Christian in the historical-creedal sense and require evangelism, not partnership.
entries.position_rationale: Trinity is the boundary of the Christian faith (Athanasian Creed). Fellowship with groups denying it would compromise the gospel. They are evangelism targets.
entries.notes: Mormons, JW, Oneness etc. — these are evangelism targets. Trinity is non-negotiable. Cross-listed with theology-proper slot; here flagged for ecclesial-relations purposes.

entries.statement: Formal ecumenical union with the Roman Catholic Church on shared sacramental, ministerial, and gospel ground is rejected so long as Rome maintains its post-Tridentine positions on justification, the Mass as sacrifice, papal infallibility, and Marian dogmas.
entries.position_rationale: Rome's Council of Trent anathematised the Reformation gospel and has not retracted it. ECT-style co-belligerence on social issues is not the same as gospel union. Affirm friendship and dialogue with Roman Catholic individuals; deny ecclesial union.
entries.notes: Distinguish (a) personal love and engagement with Roman Catholics — affirmed — from (b) institutional ecumenical union — denied. Roman Catholic individuals may be true believers despite the institution's errors; the gospel pierces every system.

entries.statement: Denominationalism — the proliferation of human party-labels (Lutheran, Methodist, etc.) — is a regrettable historical reality, but recognition of distinct gospel-faithful traditions is realistic; Brethren conviction is to gather simply on the name of Christ without sectarian label.
entries.position_rationale: 1 Cor 1 condemns party-spirit. Brethren ideal is to gather as Christians simply, recognising all true believers. In practice, sound denominations are gospel-bearing; reject sectarianism in either direction (radical Brethren exclusivism or denominational triumphalism).
entries.notes: Open Brethren ideal: simple gathering on Christ's name. Practical reality in London: many sound churches carry denominational labels; user evaluates on substance not label. Avoid Exclusive-Brethren sectarianism.

entries.statement: Parachurch ministries (mission agencies, campus ministries, mercy organisations, training institutions) are legitimate when they support and serve the local church rather than replace it.
entries.position_rationale: NT pattern centres in local assembly but allows missionary teams (Acts 13), inter-church relief (2 Cor 8-9), and trans-local ministry. Modern parachurch is biblically defensible when accountable and church-supportive.
entries.notes: Parachurch must complement, not substitute for, the local church. Brethren tradition has produced strong missionary movements. Watch for parachurch becoming a quasi-church without elders/ordinances/discipline.

entries.statement: There will be a bodily resurrection of both the just and the unjust at the end of the age.
entries.position_rationale: Daniel 12:2, John 5:28-29, and Acts 24:15 are explicit: all the dead will rise — believers to life, unbelievers to judgment. The resurrection body is physical, glorified for believers (1 Cor 15).

entries.statement: Unbelievers face eternal, conscious punishment in hell as the just judgment of a holy God.
entries.position_rationale: Christ's own words (Matt 25:46) parallel eternal punishment with eternal life — the same Greek word. Revelation describes torment 'forever and ever' (Rev 14:11, 20:10). This is the historic, orthodox, confessional position.
entries.notes: Denied by Jehovah's Witnesses (annihilationism), Seventh-day Adventists (conditional immortality), and increasingly by some evangelicals (e.g., John Stott considered annihilationism).

entries.statement: Christ will judge all people at his return — believers to eternal life, unbelievers to eternal punishment.
entries.position_rationale: Scripture is unambiguous about a final judgment of all people, with eternal consequences (Matt 25:31-46, Rev 20:11-15). The reality of judgment grounds the urgency of the gospel.

entries.statement: Full preterism — all prophecy including the second coming was fulfilled in AD 70 — is rejected as a denial of the bodily return of Christ.
entries.position_rationale: Full preterism denies the future bodily return of Christ, the future bodily resurrection, and the future final judgment. This crosses from in-house disagreement into heresy. Partial preterism (some prophecy fulfilled in AD 70, but a future second coming) is orthodox.
entries.notes: Distinguish from partial preterism (R.C. Sproul, Gentry) which is orthodox. Full preterism (hyper-preterism) collapses Christian hope.

entries.statement: Jesus Christ will return personally, visibly, audibly, and bodily to judge the living and the dead.
entries.position_rationale: The angels at the ascension declared Christ would return in the same manner he was taken up (Acts 1:11). Every eye will see him (Rev 1:7). This is a non-negotiable apostolic and creedal hope.
entries.notes: Spiritualized return (already happened invisibly in 1914, etc.) is taught by Jehovah's Witnesses and full preterists; both deviate from apostolic teaching.

entries.statement: Universalism — the view that all people will ultimately be saved — is rejected as contrary to Scripture and historic Christianity.
entries.position_rationale: Christ explicitly speaks of those who go to eternal punishment (Matt 25:46), of a narrow gate few find (Matt 7:13-14), and of the unforgivable sin (Matt 12:31-32). Universalism collapses the urgency of the gospel and was condemned in connection with Origenism at Constantinople II (553).
entries.notes: Includes hopeful universalism (Bell, Hart) and dogmatic universalism (Unitarian Universalism, Christian Universalists). Removes the necessity of conscious faith in Christ.

entries.statement: Annihilationism (the view that the wicked are ultimately destroyed and cease to exist) is rejected as inconsistent with biblical teaching on eternal punishment.
entries.position_rationale: Matt 25:46's grammatical parallelism between eternal life and eternal punishment requires both to be of the same duration. While some orthodox figures (Stott, Wenham, Fudge) have held conditionalism, the historic and confessional position is eternal conscious torment.
entries.notes: Held by some genuinely orthodox evangelicals (Stott, Pinnock, Fudge); however, when paired with Adventism or JW it is part of a wider heterodox or cultic system. Distinguish the doctrine from the holders.

entries.statement: Christ's return is imminent and could occur at any time, but no one knows the day or the hour, and date-setting is forbidden.
entries.position_rationale: Christ explicitly says 'no one knows the day or hour, not even the Son' (Matt 24:36). Date-setters (Miller 1844, Camping 2011, JW dates) all fail and bring reproach. Watchfulness, not calculation, is the posture.
entries.notes: Date-setting movements are typically cult-adjacent (Millerism, Watch Tower 1914/1925/1975, Camping).

entries.statement: Believers who die are immediately present with the Lord, conscious and at rest, awaiting the resurrection of the body.
entries.position_rationale: Paul says to depart is to be with Christ (Phil 1:23) and to be absent from the body is to be at home with the Lord (2 Cor 5:8). The thief is told 'today you will be with me in paradise' (Luke 23:43).

entries.statement: God will create new heavens and a new earth in which righteousness dwells, the eternal home of the redeemed.
entries.position_rationale: The hope of believers is not a disembodied heaven but a renewed cosmos (Isa 65, 2 Pet 3, Rev 21). Creation itself will be liberated from corruption (Rom 8).
entries.notes: A purely spiritual / non-material final state misreads biblical hope; resurrection bodies require a renewed creation.

entries.statement: Purgatory — a post-mortem place of purification for believers — is rejected as having no biblical basis.
entries.position_rationale: Christ's atonement is fully sufficient (Heb 10:14). To be absent from the body is to be present with the Lord (2 Cor 5:8), not in a purifying fire. The doctrine is built on apocryphal texts (2 Maccabees) and tradition, not Scripture.
entries.notes: Held by Roman Catholicism. Reformation-era articles explicitly reject it as 'a fond thing vainly invented.'
