entries.statement: There is a future, visible salvation of ethnic Israel as a people when 'all Israel will be saved' (Rom 11:26).
entries.position_rationale: Romans 11 explicitly forecasts Israel's future grafting back. God's covenant promises to Abraham are not annulled. This is held by both dispensationalists and many covenant theologians (e.g., Murray, Piper).
entries.notes: Replacement theology / supersessionism (the Church entirely replaces Israel) is the main alternative.

entries.statement: Prayers for the dead are rejected as having no biblical warrant and as undermining the finality of judgment.
entries.position_rationale: Hebrews 9:27 fixes judgment after death; no biblical example endorses praying for the deceased's eternal state. The practice presumes purgatory or an alterable post-mortem condition.
entries.notes: Practiced by Roman Catholics and Eastern Orthodox; tied to purgatory and intercession of saints.

entries.statement: Soul sleep — the view that the dead are unconscious until the resurrection — is rejected.
entries.position_rationale: Paul prefers death because it brings him into Christ's presence (Phil 1:23), which is meaningless if death is unconsciousness. The souls under the altar in Rev 6 cry out. The thief is with Christ 'today.'
entries.notes: Held by Seventh-day Adventists, Jehovah's Witnesses, and a minority of Reformers (psychopannychism). When part of an SDA/JW system, it is heterodox or cultic in context.

entries.statement: Amillennialism — the millennium is symbolic of the present church age between Christ's two advents — is a legitimate orthodox position.
entries.position_rationale: Held by Augustine, the magisterial Reformers, and most Reformed confessions. Has serious exegetical support, especially in handling Revelation symbolically. User leans premillennial but recognizes amillennialism's historic credentials.
entries.notes: Dominant position in confessional Reformed and Lutheran churches.

entries.statement: Classical dispensationalism — distinct programs for Israel and the Church across multiple dispensations — is a Brethren-origin system held loosely.
entries.position_rationale: The Brethren movement (Darby) is the historical origin of modern dispensationalism. It rightly preserves a future for ethnic Israel (Rom 11) and a literal hermeneutic for prophecy. However, its sharp Israel/Church bifurcation and complex dispensation schemes are open to question. Progressive dispensationalism and historic premillennialism offer balanced alternatives.
entries.notes: Covenant theology (Reformed) is the major orthodox alternative. Both are in-house Christian systems.

entries.statement: Postmillennialism — Christ returns after a long age of gospel triumph — is a legitimate orthodox position though less likely.
entries.position_rationale: Held by Puritans, Jonathan Edwards, B.B. Warfield. Has merit in taking the Great Commission seriously, but the present trajectory of the world and Scripture's apostasy warnings (2 Tim 3, Matt 24) make it less compelling than premill.
entries.notes: Often associated with Christian Reconstructionism (theonomy); in itself it is orthodox.

entries.statement: Post-tribulation rapture — the church is gathered to Christ at his visible return after the tribulation — has substantial scriptural support.
entries.position_rationale: Matt 24:29-31 places the gathering 'after the tribulation of those days.' This was the early church's view. The 'rapture' and 'second coming' may be one event, not two.
entries.notes: Historic premillennialism (Ladd, Carson, Grudem). Coheres with the church suffering through tribulation rather than escaping it.

entries.statement: Premillennialism — Christ returns before a literal thousand-year reign on earth — is the position best supported by Revelation 20 and Brethren tradition.
entries.position_rationale: Revelation 20's repeated 'thousand years' and the sequence of events (return, binding of Satan, reign, final rebellion, judgment) read most naturally as premillennial. This has been the dominant Brethren position since Darby.
entries.notes: In-house Christian disagreement. Amillennialism (Augustine, most Reformed) and postmillennialism (some Puritans, theonomists) are held by genuinely orthodox believers.

entries.statement: The pre-tribulation rapture — Christ secretly removes the church before a seven-year tribulation — is the historic Brethren-Darby position.
entries.position_rationale: Brethren tradition (Darby, Scofield) developed and popularized pre-trib. 1 Thess 4:13-18 and the imminence theme have weight. However, post-tribulationism has strong scriptural support (Matt 24:29-31), and the early church was largely post-trib. User holds it loosely.
entries.notes: Held by classical dispensationalists. Mid-trib, pre-wrath, post-trib, and no-rapture (collapsed into the second coming) are all held by orthodox believers.

entries.statement: Angels exist as personal, created, ministering spirits who serve God and his elect.
entries.position_rationale: Scripture from Genesis to Revelation is replete with angelic beings. Hebrews 1:14 calls them 'ministering spirits sent out to serve those who will inherit salvation.' The categories (cherubim, seraphim, archangels) are biblical.
entries.notes: Sadducees denied angels (Acts 23:8); some liberal theology demythologizes them.

entries.statement: A portion of the angels fell into rebellion under Satan and are now demons opposed to God.
entries.position_rationale: 2 Pet 2:4, Jude 6, and Rev 12:9 describe angelic rebellion. Demons are repeatedly cast out by Christ. Their existence and opposition are a non-negotiable backdrop of NT ministry.

entries.statement: Guardian angels — angels assigned to particular believers — is a permissible but not dogmatically required position.
entries.position_rationale: Matt 18:10 ('their angels in heaven') and Acts 12:15 suggest some kind of personal angelic association. Not strong enough to dogmatize, but consistent with Scripture.
entries.notes: Praying to or invoking angels is rejected (Col 2:18). Belief in their ministry is fine.

entries.statement: Satan is a real, personal, created spiritual being who opposes God and his people.
entries.position_rationale: Christ personally addresses Satan (Matt 4), Job 1-2 portrays him as a real adversary, and 1 Pet 5:8 calls believers to resist him. Reducing Satan to a metaphor for evil contradicts Christ's own treatment of him.
entries.notes: Christadelphians and some liberal traditions reduce Satan to a personification of human evil; this is heterodox.

entries.statement: A genuine believer, indwelt by the Holy Spirit, cannot be possessed (inhabited) by a demon, though believers can be oppressed, tempted, and harassed.
entries.position_rationale: Greater is he who is in you than he who is in the world (1 John 4:4). Believers are temples of the Holy Spirit (1 Cor 6:19). Demonic oppression and harassment of believers is biblical (Eph 6:10-18, 2 Cor 12:7); full possession is not.
entries.notes: Charismatic/NAR deliverance ministries often teach Christians can be possessed and require ongoing exorcism; this is rejected.

entries.statement: Deliverance ministry is biblically warranted in genuine cases of demonic possession but is open to abuse and requires great caution.
entries.position_rationale: Christ and the apostles cast out demons; this authority continues in principle. However, modern 'deliverance ministries' often manufacture demons, attribute psychological/medical issues to spirits, and traumatize believers. Brethren tradition is historically cautious; user holds it open with caution.
entries.notes: Avoid 'generational curse' breaking, territorial spirit warfare, and showy exorcism ministries. Genuine cases handled with prayer, Scripture, and pastoral care.

entries.statement: Unbelievers can be subject to demonic possession (full inhabitation), as the Gospels and Acts repeatedly attest.
entries.position_rationale: The Gospels record Jesus casting out demons from many; Acts continues this pattern. Modern missionary testimony, especially in animistic contexts (including India), corroborates that genuine possession occurs.

entries.statement: No 20th-century individual (William Branham) is the foretold end-time prophet or 'Elijah'; the canon is closed and no human revelation supplements Scripture.
entries.position_rationale: Branham claimed to be the prophet of the 'seventh angel' of Rev 10, taught the 'Serpent Seed' doctrine, denied the Trinity, and made false prophecies. Multiple cult-grade errors plus authoritarian elevation of a single man.
entries.notes: Branhamism / William Branham / 'Message' churches — Branham as end-time prophet, Serpent Seed (Eve's sin was sexual), Oneness Pentecostal, predestination distortions. Cult.

entries.statement: Christian sexual ethics and the protection of children are non-negotiable; movements practicing 'flirty fishing' or sexualizing evangelism or children are cults.
entries.position_rationale: Children of God / Family International (David Berg) practiced 'flirty fishing' (sexual evangelism), sexual abuse of minors documented, and Berg's 'Mo Letters' as ongoing revelation. Combines doctrinal heresy with predatory sexual abuse. Definitive cult.
confessional_anchors: Westminster Larger Catechism on the Seventh Commandment
entries.notes: Children of God / The Family / Family International (David Berg) — sexual abuse, 'flirty fishing,' Mo Letters as ongoing revelation. Criminal cult.

entries.statement: Sin, sickness, suffering, and physical reality are real; salvation is from real sin to real eternal life through Christ's real death and resurrection.
entries.position_rationale: Genesis 1 affirms creation as good and material. Christ took on real flesh (John 1:14) and bore real sin (1 Pet 2:24). Christian Science's denial of physical reality, sin, and the need for salvation is a Gnostic-style heresy that empties the gospel.
entries.notes: Denied by Christian Science (Mary Baker Eddy): sin, sickness, and matter are illusions. Refusal of medical care has caused deaths. A definitive cult.

entries.statement: Salvation is by grace alone through faith alone in Christ alone; baptism is an outward sign of inward faith, not the instrument of regeneration; and 'discipling' must not become spiritual abuse.
entries.position_rationale: Eph 2:8-9 — by grace, through faith, not of works. The thief on the cross was saved without baptism. ICC adds baptism as necessary for salvation AND requires submission to a 'discipler' for every life decision — combining works-salvation with cultic control.
confessional_anchors: all Reformation confessions on sola fide
entries.notes: International Churches of Christ (ICC, Boston Movement, Kip McKean) — baptismal regeneration plus authoritarian discipling. Classified as a cult by virtually all countercult ministries. Distinguish from mainline Churches of Christ (Restoration Movement) which, while holding baptismal regeneration, lack the cult dynamics.

entries.statement: Jesus Christ is fully God and the only mediator between God and man; no human founder of a 20th-century church (Felix Manalo) is God's last messenger.
entries.position_rationale: INC denies the Trinity, denies Christ's deity, and elevates Felix Manalo as the foretold 'angel from the east' and final messenger. This combines Arianism with extra-biblical revelation. The faith was once for all delivered (Jude 3).
entries.notes: Iglesia ni Cristo (Philippines, Felix Manalo, 1914) — denies Trinity, denies Christ's deity, claims Manalo as God's last messenger. Cult-grade. Also block-voting and authoritarian governance.

entries.statement: Jesus Christ rose bodily from the dead in the same physical body that was crucified, now glorified.
entries.position_rationale: Christ shows his disciples his hands and side (John 20:27), eats fish (Luke 24:42-43), and explicitly says 'a spirit does not have flesh and bones as you see I have' (Luke 24:39). The resurrection of the same body is the cornerstone of the gospel (1 Cor 15).
entries.notes: Denied by Jehovah's Witnesses (Christ raised as a spirit creature, not in his body). Also denied by Christian Science (no physical resurrection) and various liberal/progressive groups.

entries.statement: Jesus Christ is fully and uniquely God, eternally begotten and not created, equal in essence with the Father.
entries.position_rationale: John 1:1, John 8:58, John 20:28, Col 2:9, Heb 1:8 — Christ is unambiguously God. The Arian view (Christ as a created being or 'a god') was condemned at Nicaea (325). The NWT's 'a god' in John 1:1 is a tendentious mistranslation.
entries.notes: Denied by Jehovah's Witnesses (Watch Tower) — Christ as Michael the archangel, a created being. Also denied by Christadelphians, Iglesia ni Cristo, The Way International, and Unitarians.

entries.statement: Scripture (the 66-book canon) is closed and sufficient; no later 'scripture' (Book of Mormon, Doctrine and Covenants, Pearl of Great Price) can claim equal authority.
entries.position_rationale: Jude 3 — 'the faith once for all delivered to the saints.' Rev 22:18 forbids adding to the prophecy. Gal 1:8 — even an angel preaching another gospel is to be accursed. Joseph Smith's claimed revelations contradict and supplement Scripture.
entries.notes: Denied by Mormonism (Book of Mormon, D&C, PGP), and by extension Community of Christ and FLDS. Also at issue with Branhamism and other 'new revelation' groups.

entries.statement: Jesus Christ is the eternal Son of God, of the same substance as the Father; Satan is a created fallen angel, not Christ's spirit-brother.
entries.position_rationale: John 1:1-3 places Christ as the eternal Creator. Col 1:16 — all things including angels were created by him. Satan is a created being who fell; Christ is the uncreated Son. Equating them as 'spirit brothers' is blasphemy.
entries.notes: Taught by Mormonism (LDS): Jesus and Lucifer are spirit children of Heavenly Father. This denies Christ's eternal deity and is a cult-grade error.

entries.statement: There is one and only one true God, eternally and unchangeably; humans cannot become gods.
entries.position_rationale: Deut 6:4, Isa 43:10 ('before me no god was formed, nor shall there be any after me'), and 1 Tim 2:5 establish strict monotheism. Polytheism and the doctrine of human exaltation to godhood denies the Creator/creature distinction.
entries.notes: Denied by Mormonism (LDS): 'As man is, God once was; as God is, man may become' (Lorenzo Snow). LDS teaches a plurality of gods and human exaltation to godhood. This is polytheism and a definitive cult marker.

entries.statement: The Father, Son, and Holy Spirit are three distinct Persons sharing one divine essence — not three modes or roles of one Person.
entries.position_rationale: At Christ's baptism the Father speaks from heaven, the Son is in the water, the Spirit descends (Matt 3:16-17) — three distinct Persons simultaneously. Christ prays to the Father (John 17), distinct from himself. Modalism (Sabellianism) was condemned in the early church.
entries.notes: Denied by Oneness Pentecostalism (United Pentecostal Church International, T.D. Jakes historically, Apostolic groups) — Jesus is the one God manifesting in three modes. This is ancient Sabellian heresy.

entries.statement: Scripture is sufficient and the canon is closed; no modern individual (Ellen G. White) holds prophetic authority equal to or supplementing the apostles.
entries.position_rationale: Heb 1:1-2 — God has spoken finally in his Son. Jude 3 — the faith once for all delivered. Granting Ellen White 'continuing authority' or 'lesser light' status compromises sola scriptura. Many SDAs are functional Christians, but the system has cult-grade markers.
entries.notes: Seventh-day Adventism elevates Ellen G. White as a 'lesser light' / 'spirit of prophecy.' Combined with investigative judgment, soul sleep, conditional immortality, and Sabbatarianism. Some classify SDA as cult, others as heterodox Christian; the Ellen White issue is the cult marker.

entries.statement: The historic doctrine of the Trinity is biblical and binding; movements denying it (e.g., The Way International) are heretical.
entries.position_rationale: The Way International, founded by V.P. Wierwille, denies the Trinity, denies Christ's full deity, and teaches Jesus is not God. They also have authoritarian governance. Classic anti-Trinitarian cult.
entries.notes: The Way International (V.P. Wierwille, Power for Abundant Living) — denies Trinity, anti-Trinitarian, authoritarian. Cult.

entries.statement: Jesus Christ alone is the Messiah and Savior; no other person (Sun Myung Moon) is the 'second messiah' or true parent of mankind.
entries.position_rationale: Acts 4:12 — there is no other name under heaven by which we must be saved. Sun Myung Moon claimed to be the 'Lord of the Second Advent' and Jesus' mission was incomplete. This is anti-Christ doctrine.
entries.notes: Unification Church / Moonies (Sun Myung Moon) — Moon as second messiah, mass weddings, syncretism. Definitive cult.

entries.statement: There is one God eternally existing in three coequal, coeternal Persons — Father, Son, and Holy Spirit.
entries.position_rationale: The Trinity is the fundamental Christian doctrine of God. Matt 28:19 (one name, three persons), 2 Cor 13:14, John 1:1, Acts 5:3-4 (Holy Spirit = God) establish it. Denial places one outside Christianity.
entries.notes: Denied by Unitarian Universalism, Iglesia ni Cristo, The Way International, Christadelphians, Jehovah's Witnesses, and Oneness Pentecostals (modalism).

entries.statement: There is no biblical or genealogical basis for identifying modern Anglo-Saxons or Europeans as the lost tribes of Israel; Christian Identity racialism is heretical.
entries.position_rationale: Gal 3:28 — neither Jew nor Greek, all one in Christ. The 'lost tribes' returned in part and were absorbed; modern Israel is a mixed people. Christian Identity weaponizes this into white supremacy and antisemitism — both heretical and morally evil.
entries.notes: British Israelism / Christian Identity / Worldwide Church of God (Herbert W. Armstrong, in original form). When combined with white supremacy (Christian Identity movement) it crosses into cult-grade. Armstrongism in original form was anti-Trinitarian and cult-classified.

entries.statement: Grace empowers believers to walk in holiness; the moral law remains binding as the rule of life for the believer, though not as the means of justification.
entries.position_rationale: Rom 6:1-2 — shall we sin that grace may abound? By no means. The Sermon on the Mount intensifies, not abolishes, moral demand. Hyper-grace teachers (e.g., Joseph Prince in popular form) sometimes deny the need for confession of sin or repentance, which is antinomianism.
confessional_anchors: Heidelberg Catechism (third use of the law)
entries.notes: Hyper-grace / antinomianism — historically condemned (Marrow Controversy). Modern popular teachers vary in degree. Distinguish genuine free-grace teaching from antinomian distortion.

entries.statement: Veneration of icons (proskynesis) and prayers to saints are rejected as violating the second commandment and Christ's unique mediation.
entries.position_rationale: Ex 20:4-5 forbids making and bowing before images. While Eastern Orthodoxy distinguishes veneration (dulia) from worship (latria), in practice the second commandment is violated. 1 Tim 2:5 — one mediator. The Brethren and Reformed traditions have always rejected icon veneration.
entries.notes: Eastern Orthodoxy and Roman Catholicism. EO is heterodox (not cult) — preserves Trinity, deity of Christ, bodily resurrection. Iconoclast controversy (8th-9th c.) settled in EO favor at Nicaea II; Reformers rejected this.

entries.statement: The office of apostle in the foundational, Scripture-authoring sense ended with the apostles of Christ; modern 'apostles' and 'prophets' do not hold binding authority over the church.
entries.position_rationale: Eph 2:20 — the church is built on the foundation of the apostles and prophets, foundations are laid once. Apostles required to have seen the risen Christ (Acts 1:21-22, 1 Cor 9:1). Modern self-appointed apostles (Wagner, Hamon, Bickle, etc.) lack credentials and produce false prophecy.
entries.notes: New Apostolic Reformation (NAR) — C. Peter Wagner, Bill Hamon, Mike Bickle (IHOPKC), Bethel/Bill Johnson, Che Ahn, Lou Engle. Often combined with prosperity, dominionism (Seven Mountain Mandate), and false prophecy. Highly relevant in London charismatic scene.

entries.statement: God exhaustively foreknows all future events including free human choices; open theism's denial of God's exhaustive foreknowledge is rejected.
entries.position_rationale: Isa 46:9-10 — God declares the end from the beginning. The whole prophetic enterprise requires exhaustive foreknowledge. Open theism (Pinnock, Boyd, Sanders) limits God's omniscience and was condemned by ETS in spirit if not formally.
entries.notes: Open theism (Clark Pinnock, Greg Boyd, John Sanders) — denies exhaustive foreknowledge. Considered heterodox by virtually all confessional traditions; some (e.g., Bruce Ware) call it heretical.

entries.statement: No human bishop or council possesses infallibility ex cathedra; Scripture alone is the infallible rule of faith and life.
entries.position_rationale: Sola scriptura. The papacy itself is a post-apostolic development; even Peter was rebuked by Paul (Gal 2:11). Vatican I (1870) defined papal infallibility on faith and morals — a novel claim with no apostolic warrant.
entries.notes: Roman Catholicism. The split with Eastern Orthodoxy (1054) was partly over papal claims; Reformation reaffirmed Scripture's supremacy.

entries.statement: The gospel is the good news of forgiveness of sins through Christ; God does not guarantee health and material wealth in this life to believers.
entries.position_rationale: Christ promised tribulation in this world (John 16:33). Paul lists his sufferings (2 Cor 11) — the prosperity gospel would call him a man of small faith. The 'little gods' doctrine borders on cult-grade. Paul-Bunyan-style faith preachers (Hagin, Copeland, Hinn, etc.) distort the gospel into a transactional health-and-wealth scheme.
entries.notes: Prosperity gospel / Word-Faith (Kenneth Hagin, Kenneth Copeland, Benny Hinn, Joel Osteen, Creflo Dollar, T.D. Jakes). Some forms (e.g., 'little gods' doctrine) approach cult-grade. Pervasive in NAR and global Pentecostalism. Highly relevant for evaluating London charismatic churches.

entries.statement: Mary was a sinner saved by grace like every other believer; she was not immaculately conceived, was not bodily assumed, and is not co-redemptrix.
entries.position_rationale: Rom 3:23 — all have sinned. Christ is the one mediator (1 Tim 2:5). The Marian dogmas (Immaculate Conception 1854, Assumption 1950, Co-redemptrix proposed) lack scriptural basis and obscure Christ's unique mediation.
entries.notes: Roman Catholicism — heterodox not cult, because RC retains Trinity, deity of Christ, bodily resurrection, and (in principle) salvation by grace through Christ. But Marian dogmas, papal infallibility, and treasury of merit are serious errors. Many genuine Christians are in the RC church despite the system.

entries.statement: The gospel is to be freely offered to all people; God commands all to repent and believe; hyper-Calvinism's denial of the free offer of the gospel is rejected.
entries.position_rationale: Acts 17:30 — God commands all people everywhere to repent. 2 Pet 3:9 — not wishing any should perish. Christ wept over Jerusalem. Hyper-Calvinism's refusal to call sinners to faith because they may not be elect contradicts the apostolic pattern.
entries.notes: Hyper-Calvinism (some Primitive Baptists, some Gospel Standard Baptists) — denies duty-faith and the free offer. Distinct from confessional Calvinism.

entries.statement: Physical healing is guaranteed in the atonement for every believer in this life (such that lack of healing implies lack of faith).
entries.position_rationale: Isaiah 53 / Matthew 8 cite atonement healing eschatologically and not as a present-life guarantee; saints in Scripture suffered ongoing illness.
entries.notes: Dangerous pastorally — wounds the sick by blaming their faith.

entries.statement: Hyper-charismatic excess — slain in the Spirit as normative, holy laughter, Toronto Blessing phenomena, Bethel/NAR-style signs, fire tunnels, prophetic decrees binding consciences — is to be rejected as disorderly and unscriptural.
entries.position_rationale: 1 Cor 14 demands order; the fruit of these movements is frequently false prophecy, manipulation, and undermining of Scripture's sufficiency.
entries.notes: Visit only with sharp discernment, e.g., for evaluation. Member=false. Distinguish from moderate continuationism above.

entries.statement: The office of Apostle (in the foundational sense — eyewitness of the risen Christ, sent with Christ's authority to lay the church's foundation) ceased with the apostolic generation.
entries.position_rationale: Apostleship requires direct commissioning by the risen Christ and eyewitness status (Acts 1:21-22, 1 Cor 9:1). The church is built on the foundation of the apostles, not continually re-founded. NAR claims of restored apostles are heterodox.
entries.notes: Functional 'apostolic' missionary work (church-planting) is fine; claims to apostolic office and authority are not.

entries.statement: Any modern claim to prophecy must be tested by Scripture and is never binding on conscience the way Scripture is. New revelation that adds to or contradicts Scripture is rejected.
entries.position_rationale: Sufficiency of Scripture and the closed canon. 1 Thess 5:20-21 commands testing. Deut 18 standard for prophets is high — false prophecy is judged.
entries.notes: Cult-marker if a 'prophet' demands Scripture-equal obedience.

entries.statement: Speaking in tongues is the necessary evidence of Spirit baptism and/or genuine salvation (classical Pentecostal initial-evidence doctrine).
entries.position_rationale: Scripture distributes gifts as the Spirit wills (1 Cor 12:11, 30); tongues are not universal. Conditioning assurance on tongues introduces a works-tongue test alien to the gospel.
entries.notes: Adds a condition to salvation/assurance beyond faith in Christ. This is the classical Pentecostal extreme; many modern Pentecostals have softened it.

entries.statement: Word of Faith / 'name it and claim it' / decreeing and declaring as a creative force of human speech is a false gospel that confuses the creature with the Creator.
entries.position_rationale: Treats human speech as having God-like creative authority; shifts faith from God to the believer's own words; distorts the atonement and sovereignty of God.
entries.notes: In its most aggressive form (humans as 'little gods' speaking realities into existence), this edges into cult-grade theology — cult_marker=true. evangelism_target=true because consistent WoF teaching presents a different gospel.

entries.statement: Cessationism: the miraculous sign gifts (apostleship, prophecy, tongues, miraculous healings) ceased with the apostolic age and the closing of the canon.
entries.position_rationale: Brethren tradition is divided; my own formation included teachers leaning cessationist and others open. Personally cautious — affirm the sufficiency of Scripture and orderly worship, while acknowledging God's sovereign freedom to act.
entries.notes: Brethren formation has varied teachers on this. A moderate, orderly continuationist church is acceptable; the line is drawn at hyper-charismatic excess and prophecy treated as binding.

entries.statement: Orderly continuationism: the Spirit may still distribute miraculous gifts today, but they must be tested by Scripture and exercised with order, not displacing the sufficiency of the Word.
entries.position_rationale: Open as a position to live alongside; the abuses lie in unchecked practice, not in the bare claim that God can still gift.
entries.notes: Acceptable when paired with Scripture sufficiency, elder oversight, and 1 Cor 14 order. User's fruit-axis applies: warm orderly continuationist > cold cessationist when other essentials are intact.

entries.statement: Demonic activity is real and Christians may need pastoral care including prayer for deliverance, but routinized 'deliverance ministry' that locates a demon behind every sin and trauma is unbalanced.
entries.position_rationale: Affirm spiritual realities; deny commercialized or formulaic deliverance industries.
entries.notes: Discern between sober pastoral practice and theatrical excess.

entries.statement: God can and sometimes does heal in answer to prayer; healing is a sovereign mercy, not a guaranteed outcome.
entries.position_rationale: Scripture commands prayer for the sick and records God answering. Yet Paul's thorn, Trophimus left sick, Timothy's stomach ailment all show no guarantee.
entries.notes: Pray boldly, trust God's wisdom, use medicine.

entries.statement: The biblical gift of tongues, as exhibited at Pentecost, is the Spirit-given ability to speak real human languages previously unknown to the speaker, for the sake of intelligible witness.
entries.position_rationale: Acts 2 explicitly identifies tongues as known languages understood by hearers. Modern unintelligible vocalizations do not match the biblical pattern.
entries.notes: Distinct from the modern 'private prayer language' practice.

entries.statement: A seeker-sensitive worship design that downplays Scripture, sin, judgment, or the cross to attract unbelievers compromises worship's God-ward orientation.
entries.position_rationale: Worship is for God and the gathered saints; evangelism happens through clear gospel. Seeker-driven softening tends to produce a weaker word.
entries.notes: Distinguish from 'culturally accessible' (good) vs. 'gospel-thinning' (bad).

entries.statement: Christian worship rightly centers on the public reading and preaching of Scripture, alongside prayer, song, and the Lord's Supper.
entries.position_rationale: Apostolic pattern (Acts 2:42; 1 Tim 4:13). Word-and-Table is the historic shape.
entries.notes: Brethren historically have a strong table-centered service alongside the Word; both are essential.

entries.statement: Weekly observance of the Lord's Supper (the breaking of bread) is the New Testament and historic Brethren pattern.
entries.position_rationale: Acts 20:7 'on the first day of the week we gathered to break bread'; Brethren distinctive.
entries.notes: User's tension between doctrinally precise but cold churches vs. 80-90% correct but warm churches means preference-level differences should NOT outweigh demonstrable Christian fruit. Monthly or quarterly communion in an otherwise faithful warm church is acceptable for membership.

entries.statement: Congregational singing — the people of God lifting voices together — is the primary musical mode of Christian worship.
entries.position_rationale: NT commands singing to one another (Eph 5, Col 3); congregational voice, not performance, is the apostolic pattern.
entries.notes: Performance-band-heavy worship that drowns out the congregation is a preference issue but worth flagging.

entries.statement: Instrumentation in worship is a matter of Christian liberty; simple instrumentation or a cappella worship is honored historically in Brethren practice but not binding on others.
entries.position_rationale: Scripture has both instrumental (Psalms) and silent precedents; NT doesn't legislate.
entries.notes: Preference-level. Fruit axis applies: warm congregational singing with full band > cold austere a cappella.

entries.statement: A formal liturgy or lectionary is not commanded but is a permissible Christian-liberty matter when it preserves the gospel and Scripture's primacy.
entries.position_rationale: Brethren tradition is non-liturgical but the practice itself is not heterodox.
entries.notes: Preference-level. Fruit axis applies.

entries.statement: Singing psalms, hymns, and spiritual songs — including doctrinally rich historic hymns and biblically grounded modern songs — is good and commanded.
entries.position_rationale: Eph 5:19 / Col 3:16 explicitly includes the variety; doctrinally robust singing forms the people.
entries.notes: Reject exclusive psalmody as a binding rule; affirm psalm-singing as healthy.

entries.statement: Pro-choice ethics — that elective abortion is morally neutral or a Christian-liberty matter — is incompatible with biblical anthropology and the sixth commandment.
entries.position_rationale: Denies Imago Dei in the unborn.
entries.notes: Pastoral care for women who have had abortions is essential; the doctrinal stance is not negotiable.

entries.statement: Human life bears the image of God from conception to natural death and is to be protected; intentional taking of innocent unborn human life (abortion) is sin.
entries.position_rationale: Imago Dei from conception (Ps 139); the sixth commandment; consistent witness of historic Christianity from the Didache onward.
entries.notes: Compassion for women in crisis; refusal to call evil good. A church teaching pro-choice as Christian ethic disqualifies for membership.

entries.statement: Christians submit to civil authority as God's servant, but must obey God rather than man when laws command sin or forbid commanded obedience.
entries.position_rationale: Romans 13 and Acts 5:29 in tension and harmony.
entries.notes: Pertinent in UK speech-law and ethics climate.

entries.statement: Active euthanasia and assisted suicide are wrong; allowing natural death and refusing futile treatment is permitted.
entries.position_rationale: Sixth commandment; God's sovereignty over life and death; distinct from refusing burdensome treatment.
entries.notes: Increasing UK pressure on this; baseline is firm.

entries.statement: Truth-telling, integrity in word, and restitution where wrong has been done are required Christian practice.
entries.position_rationale: Ninth commandment; Zacchaeus pattern of restitution; abundant NT teaching on honest speech.
entries.notes: Refers to teaching, not personal failure.

entries.statement: Just-war and pacifist positions are both held by faithful Christians; Brethren tradition has historically leaned toward pacifism or non-combatant service while not making it absolute.
entries.position_rationale: Brethren divided historically; Sermon on the Mount and Romans 13 both stand. Personal stance is open with sympathy for non-combatant pacifism.
entries.notes: Brethren divided; baseline allows either.

entries.statement: Tobacco use is a matter of liberty with health-stewardship cautions; recreational drug use (including illegal drugs and intoxication-aimed cannabis) is sin and lawbreaking.
entries.position_rationale: Bodies as temples (1 Cor 6:19); civil obedience (Rom 13); intoxication forbidden (Eph 5:18). Tobacco less clear; recreational drug use clear.
entries.notes: Medicinal use distinct.

entries.statement: Alcohol consumption in moderation is a matter of Christian liberty; drunkenness is sin. Brethren tradition often counsels abstinence or strict moderation.
entries.position_rationale: Scripture explicitly forbids drunkenness, not consumption; wedding at Cana, communion wine. Brethren custom of abstinence is wisdom-driven, not commanded.
entries.notes: Brethren historically abstinent or moderate; user's stance open with sensitivity to weaker brothers (Rom 14).

entries.statement: Capital punishment for the most serious crimes, administered by lawful civil authority with due process, is permissible (open, with prudential cautions).
entries.position_rationale: Genesis 9:6 and Romans 13 affirm the magistrate's sword; modern application is prudentially debated. Brethren divided.
entries.notes: Open question; not a fellowship dividing line.

entries.statement: Gambling for gain at another's loss is unwise and contrary to neighbor-love; it is to be avoided as a regular practice.
entries.position_rationale: Greed, exploitation of the poor, poor stewardship; not a salvation issue but consistent caution.
entries.notes: Wisdom call; not a fellowship-breaking matter unless taught approvingly with greed.

entries.statement: God created humanity male and female; biological sex is binary, given by God, and immutable. Transgender ideology — that gender identity supersedes biological sex — is to be rejected.
entries.position_rationale: Genesis 1:27 'male and female he created them'; affirmed by Christ (Matt 19:4); the body is the person's covenanted self.
entries.notes: Pastoral compassion for those experiencing dysphoria; doctrinal stance not negotiable. Pronoun policy is a separate prudential matter.

entries.statement: Marriage is a covenant union of one man and one woman, instituted by God at creation, intended for life, picturing Christ and the church.
entries.position_rationale: Genesis 2:24 — affirmed by Christ in Matt 19; Eph 5 anchors marriage in the gospel mystery.
entries.notes: Essential. Church redefining marriage cannot be a member-church.

entries.statement: Same-sex sexual activity is sin, contrary to creation design and the consistent witness of Scripture; same-sex marriage is illegitimate.
entries.position_rationale: Romans 1, 1 Corinthians 6, Leviticus 18, Genesis 1-2 — affirmed across the historic church.
entries.notes: Affirming churches cannot be member-churches. Same-sex-attracted Christians pursuing chastity are honored siblings.

entries.statement: Marriage is intended for life; divorce is permitted only in cases of sexual immorality (Matt 19) and abandonment by an unbelieving spouse (1 Cor 7); remarriage after such biblically permitted divorce is permissible.
entries.position_rationale: Christ's exception clause; Pauline privilege; Brethren reading.
entries.notes: Pastoral nuance; abuse cases handled with care under abandonment principle.

entries.statement: Pornography use is sin — adultery of the heart, exploitation of those in the industry, and corrosive of the marriage bed.
entries.position_rationale: Matthew 5:28; the consistent witness of NT sexual purity teaching.
entries.notes: About teaching, not personal struggle. Strugglers receive grace and accountability.

entries.statement: Sexual intimacy is reserved for marriage between one man and one woman; pre-marital sex (fornication) and unmarried cohabitation that includes sexual relations are sin.
entries.position_rationale: Hebrews 13:4; 1 Cor 6:18; 1 Thess 4:3-5.
entries.notes: Cohabiting believers should be counseled toward marriage or separation; teaching otherwise is the disqualifier.

entries.statement: Singleness and celibacy are honorable callings, gifts from God, equally valued with marriage.
entries.position_rationale: 1 Cor 7; Christ's own celibacy; Matt 19:11-12.
entries.notes: Counter to evangelical idolatry of marriage and family.

entries.statement: IVF and reproductive technologies are open with serious ethical concerns about embryo destruction, selection, and surrogacy detached from marriage.
entries.position_rationale: Open in principle; the operative concerns are embryo personhood, number created, disposition of unused embryos, third-party gametes.
entries.notes: Couple-by-couple pastoral discernment.

entries.statement: Non-abortifacient contraception is a matter of Christian liberty; abortifacient methods (those that prevent implantation of a fertilized egg) are inconsistent with sanctity-of-life ethics.
entries.position_rationale: Brethren generally open (vs. Catholic position); the line is drawn at any method that ends a fertilized human life.
entries.notes: Brethren generally open; sanctity-of-life concern about abortifacient mechanisms.

entries.statement: Modesty in dress and self-presentation, without legalism, is a Christian virtue rooted in love of neighbor and godly self-restraint.
entries.position_rationale: 1 Tim 2:9-10; 1 Pet 3:3-4 — emphasizes inner adornment without forbidding outward care.
entries.notes: Cultural application varies; Brethren tradition often more conservative.

entries.statement: Complementarianism: men and women are equal in worth and dignity, with distinct, complementary roles; the offices of elder/overseer and the function of authoritative church teaching are reserved for qualified men. Husbands have headship; wives respond with willing submission, all in mutual love and Christ-likeness.
entries.position_rationale: Brethren tradition affirms; 1 Tim 2:12, Titus 1, Eph 5, 1 Pet 3.
entries.notes: member_if_taught_otherwise depends on the form: an egalitarian church with a female senior pastor preaching authoritatively to the gathered church = member=false. Egalitarianism only at the deacon level or in mixed teaching contexts = member=open with caution. The fruit axis applies with discernment.

entries.statement: Egalitarianism — that all church offices and authoritative-teaching functions are open to women, and that no role distinction obtains in marriage — is held by some faithful Christians but is not the position of this baseline.
entries.position_rationale: Reads NT teaching role-restrictions as cultural rather than creational; Brethren disagree.
entries.notes: Faithful Christian disagreement. Membership criteria as in complementarianism note.

entries.statement: Fathers bear primary responsibility for the spiritual formation of their children, with mothers; family worship and household catechesis are normal Christian practice.
entries.position_rationale: Deut 6:6-9; Eph 6:4; Brethren strong on family worship.
entries.notes: Single-parent and absent-father situations addressed pastorally.

entries.statement: Loving, measured corporal discipline of young children is permitted by Scripture; abusive practice is sinful and unlawful. Cultural and legal contexts shape application.
entries.position_rationale: Proverbs commends the rod; cultural and legal application varies; UK law restricts severely.
entries.notes: Cultural/legal sensitive; not a fellowship dividing line.

entries.statement: Church finances should be handled with integrity, transparency, plural oversight, and openness to the congregation.
entries.position_rationale: 2 Cor 8:18-21 — Paul's careful handling 'in the sight of the Lord but also in the sight of man.'
entries.notes: Opaque finances are a serious red flag and a frequent vector of cultic abuse.

entries.statement: The prosperity gospel — the teaching that financial wealth and physical health are guaranteed for the faithful, often unlocked by giving (seed-faith) — is a false gospel.
entries.position_rationale: Contradicts the cross, the example of Christ and the apostles, and the New Testament teaching on suffering, contentment, and the eschatological hope. Exploits the poor.
entries.notes: Consistent prosperity preaching is a different gospel — evangelism_target=true. Heterodox rather than cultic per se, but functionally devastating.

entries.statement: Christians are called to generous, proportional, cheerful giving from a willing heart; the mandatory 10% tithe of the Mosaic covenant is not directly binding on the church, though tithing is a useful baseline.
entries.position_rationale: Brethren typically deny mandatory 10% as binding law and affirm grace giving; 2 Cor 8-9 establishes the NT pattern.
entries.notes: Reject manipulative tithe-pressure tactics that promise prosperity.

entries.statement: The church is to financially support its elders who labor in the word and its missionaries sent out for the gospel.
entries.notes: Brethren historically have a strong missionary-support culture.

entries.statement: Biblical justice — care for the poor, the orphan, the widow, the oppressed — is a Christian duty; but ideological 'social justice' that supplants the gospel of forgiveness in Christ with group-based justice is a different gospel.
entries.position_rationale: Brethren affirm justice as biblical (Mic 6:8) but deny social-justice-as-gospel substitution. Galatians anathema applies to any other gospel.
entries.notes: Distinguish: justice ministry = good; gospel-as-justice = false gospel.

entries.statement: Christian nationalism — the fusion of Christian identity with national identity such that the nation-state is treated as a vehicle of Christ's kingdom — is to be regarded with caution and largely rejected.
entries.position_rationale: Christ's kingdom is not of this world (John 18:36); the church is multi-ethnic by design (Rev 5:9).
entries.notes: Distinguish from healthy patriotism / civic responsibility.

entries.statement: Persecution and suffering for Christ are normal, expected aspects of Christian discipleship in this age.
entries.notes: Counter to prosperity expectations.

entries.statement: Caring for creation as God's stewards is a Christian duty, distinct from creation-worship or pantheist environmentalism.
entries.position_rationale: Gen 1:28 / 2:15 — dominion as stewardship.
entries.notes: Reject creation-worship; affirm stewardship.

entries.statement: Christians are free to engage politically — voting, advocacy, public service — as a form of neighbor-love, while the church's mission is the gospel and not partisan alignment.
entries.position_rationale: Liberty in application; the church qua church preaches Christ.
entries.notes: Pulpit politicization in either partisan direction is a flag.

entries.statement: Public schooling, Christian schooling, and homeschooling are all permissible Christian approaches; parental responsibility for discipleship remains primary regardless.
entries.position_rationale: Brethren open. Parents bear the discipleship burden in every model.
entries.notes: Open question; not a fellowship dividing line.

entries.statement: The first day of the week, the Lord's Day, is the appointed Christian gathering day in honor of Christ's resurrection.
entries.position_rationale: Apostolic pattern (Acts 20:7; 1 Cor 16:2; Rev 1:10).
entries.notes: Distinguish from strict Sabbatarianism.

entries.statement: Mandatory Saturday Sabbath observance for Christians (Seventh-Day Adventism, Hebrew Roots) is rejected; the moral principle of one-in-seven rest stands, but binding the Saturday Sabbath on Christians is contrary to NT teaching.
entries.position_rationale: Col 2:16-17; Rom 14:5-6; Galatians warning against returning to ceremonial law.
entries.notes: Distinguish from Christians who simply gather Saturday for cultural reasons; the issue is teaching it as binding.

entries.statement: Observance of Christmas and Easter (Resurrection Sunday) is permissible Christian liberty; Brethren tradition is divided, with many observing and some rejecting the calendar as Roman accretion.
entries.position_rationale: Rom 14:5-6 — each fully convinced. Brethren divided.
entries.notes: Brethren divided; user open.

entries.statement: Active participation in occult or death-glorifying aspects of Halloween is rejected; harmless cultural participation (costumes, neighbor-friendly engagement) is a matter of conscience.
entries.position_rationale: Avoid occult (Deut 18); cultural engagement permissible (1 Cor 8). Many Brethren err on the cautious side.
entries.notes: Conscience matter.

entries.statement: Lent, Ash Wednesday, and saint-days are not commanded; Brethren typically do not observe. Personal fasting and reflection are commendable; treating the calendar as binding is rejected.
entries.position_rationale: Christian liberty; cautious of practices treated as obligatory or merit-bearing.
entries.notes: Saint-days as venerative practice rejected; calendar reflection acceptable.
